blocks.html_en:
My understanding comes from here, seems pretty clear to me but won't claim to be a lawyer of course:

> Ultimately, Judge William Alsup ruled that this destructive scanning operation qualified as fair use—but only because Anthropic had legally purchased the books first, destroyed each print copy after scanning, and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them. The judge compared the process to “conserv[ing] space” through format conversion and found it transformative. Had Anthropic stuck to this approach from the beginning, it might have achieved the first legally sanctioned case of AI fair use. Instead, the company’s earlier piracy undermined its position.

https://arstechnica.com/ai/2025/06/anthropic-destroyed-milli...

Based on that I get the impression it's quite literally the destruction part that makes it transformative, without it, it wouldn't have been tranformative at all.

sentences.en: My understanding comes from here, seems pretty clear to me but won't claim to be a lawyer of course:> Ultimately, Judge William Alsup ruled that this destructive scanning operation qualified as fair use—but only because Anthropic had legally purchased the books first, destroyed each print copy after scanning, and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them.
sentences.zh: 我的理解来自这里，对我来说似乎很清楚，但当然不会声称自己是律师：>最终，威廉·阿尔苏普法官裁定这种破坏性扫描操作符合合理使用的条件，但这只是因为 Anthropic 首先合法购买了这些书籍，在扫描后销毁了每个印刷版，并在内部保留了数字文件而不是分发它们。

sentences.en: The judge compared the process to “conserv[ing] space” through format conversion and found it transformative.
sentences.zh: 法官将这一过程比作通过格式转换“节省空间”，并发现它具有变革性。

sentences.en: Had Anthropic stuck to this approach from the beginning, it might have achieved the first legally sanctioned case of AI fair use.
sentences.zh: 如果Anthropic从一开始就坚持这种做法，它可能会取得第一个法律认可的人工智能合理使用案例。

sentences.en: Instead, the company’s earlier piracy undermined its position.https://arstechnica.com/ai/2025/06/anthropic-destroyed-milli...Based on that I get the impression it's quite literally the destruction part that makes it transformative, without it, it wouldn't have been tranformative at all.
sentences.zh: 相反，该公司早期的盗版行为削弱了它的地位。https://arstechnica.com/ai/2025/06/anthropic-destroyed-milli...基于此，我得到的印象是，它实际上是破坏部分使其具有变革性，没有它，它根本就不会具有变革性。

blocks.html_en:
I've read through the order again. I can't find anywhere where Alsup says the destruction was required.

He cites three cases where a conversion from one format to another (without destruction of the previous version) was ruled to be fair use. Including scanning books with the google books case. (And referenced the Napster case, where a similar argument was rejected)

Then made the following comparison.

"Here, every purchased print copy was copied in order to save storage space and to enable searchability as a digital copy. The print original was destroyed. One replaced the other. And, there is no evidence that the new, digital copy was shown, shared, or sold outside the company. This use was even more clearly transformative than those in Texaco, Google, and Sony Betamax (where the number of copies went up by at least one), and, of course, more transformative than those uses rejected in Napster (where the number went up by “millions” of copies shared for free with others)."

So it wasn't transformative because of the destruction. The destruction only made it "even more clearly transformative" than those other cases.

Like, how can destruction be required if there were previous cases where it wasn't?

The key legal point is not that Anthropic destroyed the books, but the key fact was that Anthropic didn't distribute the scanned copies. Alsup keeps returning to this point:

"But what matters most is whether the format change exploits anything the Copyright Act reserves to the copyright owner. Anthropic already had purchased permanent library copies (print ones). It did not create new copies to share or sell outside"

"But again, the replacement copy here was kept in the central library, not distributed"

The conclusion of that section doesn't even mention the destruction at all.

arstechnica isn't exactly wrong, the quote also mentioned "and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them". It just put way too much emphasis on the destruction, and not enough on the lack of distribution.

The other thing that arstechnica are missing:

Antropic didn't destroy the books because they thought it would strengthen their legal argument. They destroyed the because it's a lot cheaper and faster to scan books by ripping off their bindings and feeding the stacks of loose pages into a document scanner.

sentences.en: This use was even more clearly transformative than those in Texaco, Google, and Sony Betamax (where the number of copies went up by at least one), and, of course, more transformative than those uses rejected in Napster (where the number went up by “millions” of copies shared for free with others)."So it wasn't transformative because of the destruction.
sentences.zh: 这种用途比 Texaco、Google 和 Sony Betamax 中的用途（其中副本数量增加了至少一份）更具变革性，当然，也比 Napster 中拒绝的用途更具变革性（其中数量增加了与他人免费共享的“数百万”副本）。“因此，由于破坏，它并没有带来变革。

sentences.en: The destruction only made it "even more clearly transformative" than those other cases.Like, how can destruction be required if there were previous cases where it wasn't?The key legal point is not that Anthropic destroyed the books, but the key fact was that Anthropic didn't distribute the scanned copies.

sentences.en: It did not create new copies to share or sell outside""But again, the replacement copy here was kept in the central library, not distributed"The conclusion of that section doesn't even mention the destruction at all.arstechnica isn't exactly wrong, the quote also mentioned "and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them".
sentences.zh: 它没有创建新的副本以在外部共享或出售”“但是，这里的替换副本保存在中央图书馆中，而不是分发”该部分的结论甚至根本没有提到销毁。arstechnica 并不完全错误，引用还提到“并将数字文件保留在内部而不是分发它们”。

sentences.en: It just put way too much emphasis on the destruction, and not enough on the lack of distribution.The other thing that arstechnica are missing:Antropic didn't destroy the books because they thought it would strengthen their legal argument.
sentences.zh: 它只是过分强调销毁，而没有足够重视分发的缺乏。arstechnica 缺少的另一件事是：Antropic 没有销毁这些书，因为他们认为这会加强他们的法律论点。

sentences.en: They destroyed the because it's a lot cheaper and faster to scan books by ripping off their bindings and feeding the stacks of loose pages into a document scanner.
sentences.zh: 他们摧毁了它，因为通过撕下书籍装订并将成堆的松散页面送入文档扫描仪来扫描书籍要便宜得多且速度更快。

sentences.en: "Here, every purchased print copy was copied in order to save storage space and to enable searchability as a digital copy.
sentences.zh: “在这里，每一份购买的印刷版都会被复制，以节省存储空间并实现数字版的可搜索性。

sentences.en: The print original was destroyed.
sentences.zh: 印刷原件已被毁坏。

sentences.en: One replaced the other.
sentences.zh: 一个取代了另一个。

sentences.en: And, there is no evidence that the new, digital copy was shown, shared, or sold outside the company.
sentences.zh: 而且，没有证据表明新的数字副本在公司外部展示、共享或出售。

sentences.en: This use was even more clearly transformative than those in Texaco, Google, and Sony Betamax (where the number of copies went up by at least one), and, of course, more transformative than those uses rejected in Napster (where the number went up by “millions” of copies shared for free with others)."
sentences.zh: 这种用途甚至比 Texaco、Google 和 Sony Betamax 中的用途（其中副本数量增加了至少一份）更具变革性，当然，也比 Napster 中拒绝的用途（其中与其他人免费共享的副本数量增加了“数百万份”）更具变革性。”

blocks.html_en: So it wasn't transformative because of the destruction. The destruction only made it "even more clearly transformative" than those other cases.

sentences.en: So it wasn't transformative because of the destruction.
sentences.zh: 因此，由于破坏，它并没有发生变革。

sentences.en: The destruction only made it "even more clearly transformative" than those other cases.
sentences.zh: 这次破坏只会使它比其他案例“更加明显地具有变革性”。

blocks.html_en: Like, how can destruction be required if there were previous cases where it wasn't?

sentences.en: Like, how can destruction be required if there were previous cases where it wasn't?
sentences.zh: 例如，如果以前有不需要销毁的情况，怎么可能需要销毁呢？

blocks.html_en: The key legal point is not that Anthropic destroyed the books, but the key fact was that Anthropic didn't distribute the scanned copies. Alsup keeps returning to this point:

sentences.en: The key legal point is not that Anthropic destroyed the books, but the key fact was that Anthropic didn't distribute the scanned copies.
sentences.zh: 关键的法律要点不是 Anthropic 销毁了这些书籍，而是关键事实是 Anthropic 没有分发扫描版。

sentences.en: Alsup keeps returning to this point:
sentences.zh: Alsup 不断回到这一点：

blocks.html_en: "But what matters most is whether the format change exploits anything the Copyright Act reserves to the copyright owner. Anthropic already had purchased permanent library copies (print ones). It did not create new copies to share or sell outside"

sentences.en: "But what matters most is whether the format change exploits anything the Copyright Act reserves to the copyright owner.
sentences.zh: “但最重要的是格式更改是否利用了版权法保留给版权所有者的任何内容。

sentences.en: Anthropic already had purchased permanent library copies (print ones).
sentences.zh: Anthropic 已经购买了图书馆的永久副本（印刷版）。

sentences.en: It did not create new copies to share or sell outside"
sentences.zh: 它没有创建新的副本来分享或对外出售”

blocks.html_en: "But again, the replacement copy here was kept in the central library, not distributed"

sentences.en: "But again, the replacement copy here was kept in the central library, not distributed"
sentences.zh: “但是，这里的替换副本保存在中央图书馆，而不是分发”

blocks.html_en: The conclusion of that section doesn't even mention the destruction at all.

sentences.en: The conclusion of that section doesn't even mention the destruction at all.
sentences.zh: 该部分的结论甚至根本没有提到破坏。

blocks.html_en: arstechnica isn't exactly wrong, the quote also mentioned "and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them". It just put way too much emphasis on the destruction, and not enough on the lack of distribution.

sentences.en: arstechnica isn't exactly wrong, the quote also mentioned "and kept the digital files internally rather than distributing them".
sentences.zh: arstechnica 并不完全错误，引用还提到“并将数字文件保留在内部而不是分发它们”。

sentences.en: It just put way too much emphasis on the destruction, and not enough on the lack of distribution.
sentences.zh: 它只是过分强调破坏，而没有足够重视分配的缺乏。

blocks.html_en: The other thing that arstechnica are missing:

sentences.en: The other thing that arstechnica are missing:
sentences.zh: arstechnica 缺少的另一件事是：

blocks.html_en: Antropic didn't destroy the books because they thought it would strengthen their legal argument. They destroyed the because it's a lot cheaper and faster to scan books by ripping off their bindings and feeding the stacks of loose pages into a document scanner.

sentences.en: Antropic didn't destroy the books because they thought it would strengthen their legal argument.
sentences.zh: Antropic 没有销毁这些书，因为他们认为这会加强他们的法律论据。

sentences.en: They destroyed the because it's a lot cheaper and faster to scan books by ripping off their bindings and feeding the stacks of loose pages into a document scanner.
sentences.zh: 他们摧毁了它，因为通过撕下书籍装订并将成堆的松散页面送入文档扫描仪来扫描书籍要便宜得多且速度更快。

blocks.html_en:
Yet countless families, including old folks were ruined during untold numbers of RIAA suits because "converting to save space" is not a permissable use.

They used to go around destroying lives by the thousands after Napster was creating because of the invalidity of that argument.

It is a crime to make a CD of your MP3s and vice versa, and you cannot convert your VHS to DVD.

A billionaire does it at scale, well then saving space via format conversion is a grand, while the peons still can see their lives destroyed but with it hidden via the CCB secret panel. Two tier American Justice on full display. Bankrupty and seizure or worse for thee and billions for he. Format conversion legalized only for oligarchs, and of course, no appeal so it will only be a binding precedent on that one rich guy and nobody else. Tribe on both sides, keeping special rights for themselves that are illegal for everybody else.

sentences.en: Yet countless families, including old folks were ruined during untold numbers of RIAA suits because "converting to save space" is not a permissable use.They used to go around destroying lives by the thousands after Napster was creating because of the invalidity of that argument.It is a crime to make a CD of your MP3s and vice versa, and you cannot convert your VHS to DVD.A billionaire does it at scale, well then saving space via format conversion is a grand, while the peons still can see their lives destroyed but with it hidden via the CCB secret panel.
sentences.zh: 然而，无数的家庭，包括老人，在无数的 RIAA 诉讼中被毁了，因为“转换以节省空间”不是一种被允许的用途。在 Napster 诞生后，他们曾经到处毁掉成千上万的生命，因为这个论点是无效的。用你的 MP3 制作 CD 是一种犯罪，反之亦然，你不能将你的 VHS 转换成 DVD。亿万富翁大规模地这样做，那么通过格式转换节省空间就很了不起，而苦工仍然可以看到他们的照片。生命被摧毁，但通过 CCB 秘密小组隐藏起来。

sentences.en: Two tier American Justice on full display.
sentences.zh: 两层美国正义得到充分展示。

sentences.en: Bankrupty and seizure or worse for thee and billions for he.
sentences.zh: 你破产、没收或者更糟，他则损失数十亿美元。

sentences.en: Format conversion legalized only for oligarchs, and of course, no appeal so it will only be a binding precedent on that one rich guy and nobody else.
sentences.zh: 格式转换只对寡头合法化，当然，没有上诉，所以这只会成为对那个富人有约束力的先例，而不是其他人。

sentences.en: Tribe on both sides, keeping special rights for themselves that are illegal for everybody else.
sentences.zh: 双方部落都保留着对其他人来说都是非法的特殊权利。

blocks.html_en: This is not backed up by any evidence. Ripping CDs was never illegal. The DMCA made the circumvention of an effective copyright protection mechanism illegal, which made ripping DVDs and Blu-rays a crime. But that's separate from copyright itself. The RIAA sued Napster users not because they were converting files, but because they were obtaining them from others without a license.

sentences.en: This is not backed up by any evidence.
sentences.zh: 这没有任何证据支持。

sentences.zh: 翻录 CD 从来都不是违法的。

sentences.zh: 但这与版权本身是分开的。

sentences.zh: RIAA 起诉 Napster 用户并不是因为他们转换文件，而是因为他们在未经许可的情况下从其他人那里获取文件。

blocks.html_en:
There is a lot of evidence. I lived through it. Every family with children and an internet connection or MP3 player was terrified of getting ruined suddenly via a letter. It was in the news every day about some other grandpa or single mother losing their house.

Ripping CDs was long illegal. Perhaps the Librarian of Congress made an exception. Now they hid everything behind a CCB that is like Arbitration so we will never know because they have hid almost aspects of societal justice about copyright and business labor behind arbitration style secrecy. The most useful courts are secret and now people believe there are no proceedings and they do not understand how much of our society was litigated and debated before.

Here is an article from 2008 Specifically explaining that ripping a CD to format convert for personal use is illegal and the RIAA and Sony BMG saying it merited suit but they had bigger fish to fry.

1. https://www.npr.org/transcripts/17814972

Mr. FISHER: That's right. So then, you have to ask yourself, why is the industry continuing to cling to that notion that there is no such legal right? (1)

"Bigger fish to fry" is not the same as "legal".

People selling software to easily convert VHS to hard drive were also punished. For decades they were very clear that format shifting was outlawed. But now that it supports centralizing power and creating a permanent class of info-priests to rule the society, they allow it for them.

Frankly, making all the justice system secret is why the media had to turn to personality cult nonsense for most reporting. All the great stories of the past were informed via the justice system activities. Since all the court stuff is secret now, all they had to talk about was Donald Trump.

"Discovery" provided the bulk of news facts before they secreted away all the justice system proceedings for liability, labor, negligence, medical care, copyright.

It used to be possible to know stuff about America and there was "evidence" all over the place. Now there is never any evidence for anything anywhere. That's Scalia's legacy thanks to Concepcion, absolutely gutting the ability of the society to use Hawthorne effects to discern legality and behavior.

This country used to have evidence for everything, and now a lack of evidence is so common that it is a trope level popular refrain.

blocks.html_en:
There is a lot of evidence. I lived through it. Every family with children and an internet connection or MP3 player was terrified of getting ruined suddenly via a letter. It was in the news every day about some other grandpa or single mother losing their house.

Ripping CDs was long illegal. Perhaps the Librarian of Congress made an exception. Now they hid everything behind a CCB that is like Arbitration so we will never know because they have hid almost aspects of societal justice about copyright and business labor behind arbitration style secrecy. The most useful courts are secret and now people believe there are no proceedings and they do not understand how much of our society was litigated and debated before.

Here is an article from 2008 Specifically explaining that ripping a CD to format convert for personal use is illegal and the RIAA and Sony BMG saying it merited suit but they had bigger fish to fry.

1. https://www.npr.org/transcripts/17814972

Mr. FISHER: That's right. So then, you have to ask yourself, why is the industry continuing to cling to that notion that there is no such legal right? (1)

"Bigger fish to fry" is not the same as "legal".

People selling software to easily convert VHS to hard drive were also punished. For decades they were very clear that format shifting was outlawed. But now that it supports centralizing power and creating a permanent class of info-priests to rule the society, they allow it for them.

Frankly, making all the justice system secret is why the media had to turn to personality cult nonsense for most reporting. All the great stories of the past were informed via the justice system activities. Since all the court stuff is secret now, all they had to talk about was Donald Trump.

"Discovery" provided the bulk of news facts before they secreted away all the justice system proceedings for liability, labor, negligence, medical care, copyright.

It used to be possible to know stuff about America and there was "evidence" all over the place. Now there is never any evidence for anything anywhere. That's Scalia's legacy thanks to Concepcion, absolutely gutting the ability of the society to use Hawthorne effects to discern legality and behavior.

This country used to have evidence for everything, and now a lack of evidence is so common that it is a trope level popular refrain.

blocks.html_en:
You're confused about what was actually illegal and what the industry wanted you to believe was illegal. They didn't want to take anybody to court for actually ripping a CD because they didn't want to lose and have the precedent set like it was in Sony versus Betamax. This isn't "bigger fish to fry". This is "terrified of the precedent".

> The dispute arises from a suit the RIAA filed against a man in Arizona who bought CDs, copied them into his computer as MP3 files, and then put them into a shared folder that other people could access through Kazaa, a computer program for sharing music. He's being sued for that last part.

Then they state what they wish were true:

> But according to Marc Fisher, legal documents and some statements by industry officials make it clear that the industry regards the simple act of copying a CD onto your computer or your iPod as illegal.

But just because they wished it to be did not make it so. Trillion-dollar companies have provided end-users with software to rip CDs (including iTunes), and there's never been a court case over it.

sentences.en: You're confused about what was actually illegal and what the industry wanted you to believe was illegal.
sentences.zh: 您对什么实际上是非法的以及该行业希望您相信什么是非法的感到困惑。

sentences.en: They didn't want to take anybody to court for actually ripping a CD because they didn't want to lose and have the precedent set like it was in Sony versus Betamax.
sentences.zh: 他们不想将任何人因实际翻录 CD 而告上法庭，因为他们不想失败，也不想开创像索尼诉 Betamax 那样的先例。

sentences.en: This isn't "bigger fish to fry".
sentences.zh: 这不是“更大的鱼要煎”。

sentences.en: He's being sued for that last part.Then they state what they wish were true:> But according to Marc Fisher, legal documents and some statements by industry officials make it clear that the industry regards the simple act of copying a CD onto your computer or your iPod as illegal.But just because they wished it to be did not make it so.
sentences.zh: 他因最后一​​部分而被起诉。然后他们陈述了他们所希望的事实：> 但根据 Marc Fisher 的说法，法律文件和行业官员的一些声明清楚地表明，该行业认为将 CD 复制到您的计算机或 iPod 上的简单行为是非法的。但仅仅因为他们希望如此，并不意味着它如此。

sentences.en: Trillion-dollar companies have provided end-users with software to rip CDs (including iTunes), and there's never been a court case over it.
sentences.zh: 价值数万亿美元的公司已经向最终用户提供了翻录 CD 的软件（包括 iTunes），并且从未出现过相关的法庭案件。

blocks.html_en: You're confused about what was actually illegal and what the industry wanted you to believe was illegal. They didn't want to take anybody to court for actually ripping a CD because they didn't want to lose and have the precedent set like it was in Sony versus Betamax. This isn't "bigger fish to fry". This is "terrified of the precedent".

sentences.en: You're confused about what was actually illegal and what the industry wanted you to believe was illegal.
sentences.zh: 您对什么实际上是非法的以及该行业希望您相信什么是非法的感到困惑。

sentences.en: They didn't want to take anybody to court for actually ripping a CD because they didn't want to lose and have the precedent set like it was in Sony versus Betamax.
sentences.zh: 他们不想将任何人因实际翻录 CD 而告上法庭，因为他们不想失败，也不想开创像索尼诉 Betamax 那样的先例。

sentences.en: This isn't "bigger fish to fry".
sentences.zh: 这不是“更大的鱼要煎”。

sentences.en: This is "terrified of the precedent".
sentences.zh: 这就是“惧怕先例”。

I'm not aware of any cases where the RIAA sued people who ripped their own CD/DVDs/VHS for personal use.

Their MO was suing owners of internet connections which were seen sharing content on file sharing networks.

blocks.html_en:
Why is scanning a book transformative(a la Google) but reading data off a CD and putting into a digital format not?

How is streaming bits of the music from your computer not transformative?

blocks.html_en:
They did not change the laws. The rich tribe guy ignored them and they let him off just like the Anthropic guy. They did not change the laws. The old businesses just folded and the new ones via unlicensed independent contractors made cottage industries out of small scale fraud and tax evasion.

How many Uber drivers can show their local business license for every town they pick people up in? How many have sales tax accounts for their state? Every uber driver without them should have been charged with the same crime as Al Capone.

Now they are trying to control the knowledge, and the vehicle driving, etc. via AI.

It is frankly a tribe takeover via mass criminal activity.

He should have been charged with tax evasion for every pickup in a place where he lacked a business license, but the tribe would never allow it. Compliance is only for the other guys.

Only the dumb local guy graduating high school trying to earn a living has to worry about legal compliance since the rich guys are too hard to prosecute.

They did not change laws. They refuse to enforce them and we are being taken over by the reincarnated legion of Al Capone as a result.

sentences.en: They did not change the laws.
sentences.zh: 他们没有改变法律。

sentences.en: The rich tribe guy ignored them and they let him off just like the Anthropic guy.
sentences.zh: 富有的部落家伙无视了他们，他们就像人类一样放走了他。

sentences.en: They did not change the laws.
sentences.zh: 他们没有改变法律。

sentences.en: The old businesses just folded and the new ones via unlicensed independent contractors made cottage industries out of small scale fraud and tax evasion.How many Uber drivers can show their local business license for every town they pick people up in?
sentences.zh: 老企业刚刚倒闭，新企业通过无证独立承包商通过小规模欺诈和逃税创建了家庭手工业。有多少 Uber 司机能够在接载乘客的每个城镇出示当地营业执照？

sentences.en: How many have sales tax accounts for their state?
sentences.zh: 有多少人拥有所在州的销售税账户？

sentences.en: Every uber driver without them should have been charged with the same crime as Al Capone.Now they are trying to control the knowledge, and the vehicle driving, etc. via AI.It is frankly a tribe takeover via mass criminal activity.He should have been charged with tax evasion for every pickup in a place where he lacked a business license, but the tribe would never allow it.
sentences.zh: 每一个没有他们的超级司机都应该被指控与阿尔·卡彭同样的罪行。现在他们试图通过人工智能来控制知识、车辆驾驶等。坦率地说，这是部落通过大规模犯罪活动进行接管。他应该在没有营业执照的地方每接一次皮卡就被指控逃税，但部落绝对不会允许。

sentences.en: Compliance is only for the other guys.Only the dumb local guy graduating high school trying to earn a living has to worry about legal compliance since the rich guys are too hard to prosecute.They did not change laws.
sentences.zh: 合规只是其他人的事。只有那些高中毕业想要谋生的愚蠢的本地人才需要担心法律合规性，因为有钱人太难起诉了。他们没有改变法律。

sentences.en: They refuse to enforce them and we are being taken over by the reincarnated legion of Al Capone as a result.
sentences.zh: 他们拒绝执行这些规定，结果我们被阿尔·卡彭的转世军团接管。

blocks.html_en:
You're mistaken.

The transformativeness of the use is independent of the destruction of the books. The destruction of the books allowed them to argue that they had not duplicated them, and was instrumental in the argument supporting the legality of scanning them. But that's entirely upstream of the way the data was leveraged, which is what is critical in the argument about the use being transformative.

sentences.en: You're mistaken.The transformativeness of the use is independent of the destruction of the books.
sentences.zh: 你错了。使用的变革性与书籍的销毁无关。

sentences.en: The destruction of the books allowed them to argue that they had not duplicated them, and was instrumental in the argument supporting the legality of scanning them.
sentences.zh: 这些书的销毁使他们能够辩称自己没有复制这些书，并且有助于支持扫描这些书的合法性。

sentences.en: But that's entirely upstream of the way the data was leveraged, which is what is critical in the argument about the use being transformative.
sentences.zh: 但这完全是数据利用方式的上游，这也是有关数据使用具有变革性的争论中的关键所在。

blocks.html_en: That's a good ruling, because otherwise only the big companies can afford to pay for enough content to make an LLM (say goodbye to open weight or research LLMs). Having a fee like this is actually a form of regulatory capture.

sentences.en: That's a good ruling, because otherwise only the big companies can afford to pay for enough content to make an LLM (say goodbye to open weight or research LLMs).
sentences.zh: 这是一个很好的裁决，因为否则只有大公司才有能力支付足够的内容来获得法学硕士（告别公开体重或研究法学硕士）。

sentences.en: Having a fee like this is actually a form of regulatory capture.
sentences.zh: 收取这样的费用实际上是一种监管捕获形式。

blocks.html_en:
>But if I'm reading the ruling correctly, Anthropic might have been fine even with feeding pirated books into their LLM (as long as they planned to eventually deleted them afterwards)

You're not. Even if training is fair use, it doesn't mean you can steal copies to train the model. It just means the training itself isn't an infringement (in Alsup's opinion). Stealing the copies of the books was an infringement and that's exactly the liability that Anthropic settled.

sentences.en: >But if I'm reading the ruling correctly, Anthropic might have been fine even with feeding pirated books into their LLM (as long as they planned to eventually deleted them afterwards)You're not.
sentences.zh: >但如果我正确地阅读了裁决，即使将盗版书籍喂入他们的法学硕士（只要他们计划最终删除它们），Anthropic 也可能没问题，但你不是。

sentences.zh: 即使训练是合理使用的，也不意味着你可以窃取副本来训练模型。

sentences.en: It just means the training itself isn't an infringement (in Alsup's opinion).
sentences.zh: 这只是意味着培训本身并不构成侵权（阿尔苏普认为）。

sentences.en: Stealing the copies of the books was an infringement and that's exactly the liability that Anthropic settled.
sentences.zh: 窃取书籍副本属于侵权行为，这正是 Anthropic 所解决的责任。

blocks.html_en:
It's easier to ask for forgiveness than permission, right?

It seems to be the modus operandi of corporations in general: they commit any kind of infringement they want and then later they go for a settlement with a value that's, of course, not too big for a company too big to fail.

In the meantime, the average person or company gets shafted.

In my opinion, we are one step away from AI companies capturing the entirety of copyright legislation.

sentences.en: It's easier to ask for forgiveness than permission, right?It seems to be the modus operandi of corporations in general: they commit any kind of infringement they want and then later they go for a settlement with a value that's, of course, not too big for a company too big to fail.In the meantime, the average person or company gets shafted.In my opinion, we are one step away from AI companies capturing the entirety of copyright legislation.
sentences.zh: 请求宽恕比请求许可更容易，对吧？这似乎是一般公司的作案手法：他们想要实施任何形式的侵权，然后他们寻求和解，其价值当然对于大到不能倒的公司来说不算太大。与此同时，普通人或公司受到了伤害。在我看来，我们距离人工智能公司掌握整个版权立法仅一步之遥。

blocks.html_en:
You do indeed appear to have a valid point. Many "chosen" companies, like Uber for example, appear to have broken numerous laws. Legal action against many such companies comes suspiciously slowly, where they have already obtained massive profits and value, before the possibility of being shut down comes. Then, when they are finally pulled into court, they have all kinds of money for the best lawyers and have already paid the right politicians (and others).

When the legal judgements for wrongdoing are finally handed out, they often come across as just an inconvenience or kind of tax, which is easily handled in comparison to the profits they've already made. Yet, if average Joe or persons not considered as being of "the right type" were to do such actions, they quickly get the full book thrown at them. Often, the full measure of legal punishment, where their company and life is or about nearly over.

sentences.en: You do indeed appear to have a valid point.
sentences.zh: 看来你的观点确实有道理。

sentences.en: Many "chosen" companies, like Uber for example, appear to have broken numerous laws.
sentences.zh: 许多“被选中”的公司，例如优步，似乎违反了许多法律。

sentences.en: Legal action against many such companies comes suspiciously slowly, where they have already obtained massive profits and value, before the possibility of being shut down comes.
sentences.zh: 针对许多此类公司的法律诉讼来得缓慢得令人怀疑，在可能被关闭之前，它们已经获得了巨额利润和价值。

sentences.en: Then, when they are finally pulled into court, they have all kinds of money for the best lawyers and have already paid the right politicians (and others).When the legal judgements for wrongdoing are finally handed out, they often come across as just an inconvenience or kind of tax, which is easily handled in comparison to the profits they've already made.
sentences.zh: 然后，当他们最终被拉上法庭时，他们拥有各种各样的钱请最好的律师，并且已经付给了合适的政客（和其他人）。当对不法行为的法律判决最终下达时，他们常常被认为只是一种不便或一种税收，与他们已经赚取的利润相比，这很容易处理。

sentences.en: Yet, if average Joe or persons not considered as being of "the right type" were to do such actions, they quickly get the full book thrown at them.
sentences.zh: 然而，如果普通人或不被认为是“正确类型”的人做出这样的行为，他们很快就会收到整本书。

sentences.en: Often, the full measure of legal punishment, where their company and life is or about nearly over.
sentences.zh: 通常，他们的陪伴和生活已经或即将结束，这是全面的法律惩罚。

blocks.html_en:
Hey I just came up with this idea, I'm going to feed copyrighted books into my LLM that remembers them verbatim, and then people pay me to ask the LLM for complete copies of a book.

Wait, no, not verbatim. It transforms upper case into lower case and vice versa.

sentences.en: Hey I just came up with this idea, I'm going to feed copyrighted books into my LLM that remembers them verbatim, and then people pay me to ask the LLM for complete copies of a book.Wait, no, not verbatim.
sentences.zh: 嘿，我刚刚想到这个想法，我将把受版权保护的书籍输入我的法学硕士，逐字记住它们，然后人们付钱给我向法学硕士索要一本书的完整副本。等等，不，不是逐字记录。

sentences.en: It transforms upper case into lower case and vice versa.
sentences.zh: 它将大写字母转换为小写字母，反之亦然。

blocks.html_en:
Ideas are not protected by copyright, nor are facts. You need to have a very specific and 'creative' / 'substantial' expression of an idea for copyright to apply.

The output of an LLM can be easily be such, but usually not.

sentences.en: Ideas are not protected by copyright, nor are facts.
sentences.zh: 想法不受版权保护，事实也不受版权保护。

sentences.en: You need to have a very specific and 'creative' / 'substantial' expression of an idea for copyright to apply.The output of an LLM can be easily be such, but usually not.
sentences.zh: 您需要对申请版权的想法有一个非常具体和“创造性”/“实质性”的表达。法学硕士的输出很容易就是这样，但通常不是。

blocks.html_en:
I'll link to a previous comment of mine: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48968156

> You need to have a very specific and 'creative' / 'substantial' expression of an idea for copyright to apply. The output of an LLM can be easily be such, but usually not.

This is incomplete with current US law. You need the above (the typical copyright qualifiers) AND evidence of substantial human involvement in the creation.

Minimally directing an autonomous agent does not qualify.

blocks.html_en:
Just to be clear, what you're referring to is the current US standard for whether a work is copywritable, not whether training on data and "regurgitating existing ideas" is fair-use. The latter is what the GP comment was about:

> There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas. That is probably the correct way to legislate this. If anything a human does can instantly be copied by an LLM, and then sent to all its subscribers, things need to change

sentences.en: Just to be clear, what you're referring to is the current US standard for whether a work is copywritable, not whether training on data and "regurgitating existing ideas" is fair-use.
sentences.zh: 需要澄清的是，您指的是当前美国关于作品是否可复制的标准，而不是数据培训和“反省现有想法”是否合理使用。

sentences.en: The latter is what the GP comment was about:> There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas.
sentences.zh: 后者是 GP 评论的内容：> 需要根据人工智能是否反省现有想法来支付特许权使用费。

sentences.en: That is probably the correct way to legislate this.
sentences.zh: 这可能是立法的正确方式。

sentences.en: If anything a human does can instantly be copied by an LLM, and then sent to all its subscribers, things need to change
sentences.zh: 如果法学硕士可以立即复制人类所做的任何事情，然后发送给其所有订阅者，那么情况就需要改变

blocks.html_en:
Correct. I just wanted to clarify the statement that parent made, as it seems like lots of people have a misassumption about the copyrightability of autonomous in the United States.

Expect it will be clarified and/or changed by law given how much money is at stake, but the current state is what the current state is.

If I were developing key IP with agents, I'd be very careful to document my human contribution.

sentences.en: Correct.
sentences.zh: 正确的。

blocks.html_en:
> … very specific …

That phrase is doing a lot of work. In the US, any writing is automatically protected by copyright. (This comment, for example.) Whether the author can claim infringement is a can of worms: legal costs, fair use … but your “very specific” phrasing makes it sound like there’s a prescription for exactly what is protected by copyright - there is not.

> Ideas are not protected by copyright.

The expression of the idea is, however. Same with facts. The fact that I live at a specific street address is not protected. My sentence construction explaining my specific street address is protected.

> The output of an LLM …

… is not protected, not matter its shape. The US Copyright Office has declared as much.

sentences.en: very specific …That phrase is doing a lot of work.
sentences.zh: 非常具体……这句话做了很多工作。

sentences.zh: 在美国，任何写作都自动受到版权保护。

sentences.en: (This comment, for example.)
sentences.zh: （例如，这条评论。）

sentences.en: Whether the author can claim infringement is a can of worms: legal costs, fair use …
sentences.zh: 作者是否可以声称侵权是一个棘手的问题：法律费用、合理使用……

sentences.en: but your “very specific” phrasing makes it sound like there’s a prescription for exactly what is protected by copyright - there is not.> Ideas are not protected by copyright.The expression of the idea is, however.

sentences.en: Same with facts.
sentences.zh: 与事实相同。

sentences.en: The fact that I live at a specific street address is not protected.
sentences.zh: 我住在特定街道地址的事实不受保护。

sentences.en: My sentence construction explaining my specific street address is protected.> The output of an LLM ……
sentences.zh: 我的句子结构解释了我的特定街道地址受到保护。> 法学硕士的输出……

sentences.en: is not protected, not matter its shape.
sentences.zh: 无论其形状如何，都不受保护。

sentences.en: The US Copyright Office has declared as much.
sentences.zh: 美国版权局也声明了这一点。

blocks.html_en:
> There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas.

So, by that logic, you need to be paying every time you regurgitate any of my ideas. Or anyone else's. Copyright now protects abstractions and vibes. Substantial similarity test be damned. Nobody can write stories about wizard schools, the idea is taken.

sentences.zh: 或者其他人的。

sentences.zh: 没人能写关于巫师学校的故事，这个想法被采纳了。

blocks.html_en: Humans are not computers. Humans are not a service. In the end, all laws are made up rules and can absolutely be written to have different outcomes and restrictions based on if a human is doing something or if a program is doing it.

blocks.html_en:
Real question, if an LLM shouldn't be able to remix someone's written work, why should a robot be able to build a chair that kinda looks like a chair a carpenter built that one time? The carpenter was a human, and humans are not a service.

Why this distinction only for intellectual work?

blocks.html_en:
the "codec" is not really the point.

playing an MP3 at a venue, streaming it or distributing it is a copyrighted act because, despite not being a verbatim copy of the original material, it is capable of producing a nearly-verbatim version of that intellectual property well enough that most people won't be able to notice the difference.

similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.

the fact that an MP3 cannot "paraphrase" or "summarize" the audio data is not what makes it copyrighted, and neither does the ability of an LLM to "paraphrase" or "summarize" the textual data it's been trained on, make it any less intellectual property theft

the motivation for the audio case is the sense that the listener will not care whether the DJ plays an MP3 (they didn't pay for) or plays the original record (they would have paid for).

similarly for the lossily compressed text engine aka LLM's case, many people will not care whether they get this textual information paraphrased or nearly verbatim from an LLM trained on pirated books, or the original books.

the fact that an LLM also has the ability to paraphrase or summarize the pirated textual information it's been trained on, doesn't really matter if it's also capable of producing nearly verbatim copies of (parts of) those texts.

to underline this point even more, we know that MP3s (and more modern and much more efficient codecs like OPUS, after that) have been psycho-acoustically optimized to store exactly the least amount of data that will get "the point" of that music across to the listener, to the extent that they do not need the original recording any more. this is the stated goal of lossy compressed audio, after all. well, it also happens to be the (pretty much stated) goal of LLM companies, to store exactly the least amount of data that will get the point of that text to the reader. and it does tend to cause the readers to not really care about the original book any more.

having said all that, I don't mean to argue to lock it all up. I actually mean to argue that we should demand that Anthropic and Open AI release their weights data, and if anyone were to happen to break into them and steal that data, I would have exactly zero pity for that. because fair is fair.

sentences.en: the "codec" is not really the point.playing an MP3 at a venue, streaming it or distributing it is a copyrighted act because, despite not being a verbatim copy of the original material, it is capable of producing a nearly-verbatim version of that intellectual property well enough that most people won't be able to notice the difference.similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.the fact that an MP3 cannot "paraphrase" or "summarize" the audio data is not what makes it copyrighted, and neither does the ability of an LLM to "paraphrase" or "summarize" the textual data it's been trained on, make it any less intellectual property theftthe motivation for the audio case is the sense that the listener will not care whether the DJ plays an MP3 (they didn't pay for) or plays the original record (they would have paid for).similarly for the lossily compressed text engine aka LLM's case, many people will not care whether they get this textual information paraphrased or nearly verbatim from an LLM trained on pirated books, or the original books.the fact that an LLM also has the ability to paraphrase or summarize the pirated textual information it's been trained on, doesn't really matter if it's also capable of producing nearly verbatim copies of (parts of) those texts.to underline this point even more, we know that MP3s (and more modern and much more efficient codecs like OPUS, after that) have been psycho-acoustically optimized to store exactly the least amount of data that will get "the point" of that music across to the listener, to the extent that they do not need the original recording any more.
sentences.zh: “编解码器”并不是真正的重点。在某个场所播放 MP3、流式传输或分发它是受版权保护的行为，因为尽管不是原始材料的逐字副本，但它能够足够好地生成该知识产权的近逐字版本，以至于大多数人无法注意到其中的差异。同样，正如（许多出版商和作者）所表明的那样，法学硕士能够生成他们所接受培训的文本的近逐字版本，质量足够好，大多数人都无法注意到其中的差异。MP3 无法“解释”或“总结”音频数据的事实并不意味着它受版权保护，法学硕士“解释”或“总结”其所训练的文本数据的能力也不能减少知识产权盗窃音频案例的动机是听众不会关心 DJ 是否播放 MP3（他们没有付费）或播放原始记录（他们会付费）。类似地，对于有损压缩文本引擎（又名法学硕士）的情况，许多人不会关心他们是否从受过盗版书籍培训的法学硕士或原始书籍中获得这些文本信息的释义或几乎逐字记录。事实上，法学硕士也有能力释义或总结其所受培训的盗版文本信息，如果它也能够生成近乎逐字的副本，这一事实并不重要为了进一步强调这一点，我们知道 MP3（以及之后更现代、更高效的编解码器，如 OPUS）已经过心理声学优化，可以存储最少量的数据，从而将音乐的“要点”传达给听众，以至于他们不再需要原始录音。

sentences.en: this is the stated goal of lossy compressed audio, after all.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，这是有损压缩音频的既定目标。

sentences.en: well, it also happens to be the (pretty much stated) goal of LLM companies, to store exactly the least amount of data that will get the point of that text to the reader.
sentences.zh: 好吧，这也恰好是法学硕士公司的（几乎明确的）目标，即存储最少的数据来向读者传达文本的要点。

sentences.en: and it does tend to cause the readers to not really care about the original book any more.having said all that, I don't mean to argue to lock it all up.
sentences.zh: 这确实会导致读者不再真正关心原著。说了这么多，我并不是想说要把它全部锁起来。

sentences.en: I actually mean to argue that we should demand that Anthropic and Open AI release their weights data, and if anyone were to happen to break into them and steal that data, I would have exactly zero pity for that.
sentences.zh: 我实际上的意思是说，我们应该要求 Anthropic 和 Open AI 发布他们的权重数据，如果有人碰巧闯入他们并窃取这些数据，我对此的同情心为零。

sentences.en: because fair is fair.
sentences.zh: 因为公平就是公平。

sentences.zh: 毕竟，这是有损压缩音频的既定目标。

sentences.en: well, it also happens to be the (pretty much stated) goal of LLM companies, to store exactly the least amount of data that will get the point of that text to the reader.
sentences.zh: 好吧，这也恰好是法学硕士公司的（几乎明确的）目标，即存储最少的数据来向读者传达文本的要点。

sentences.en: and it does tend to cause the readers to not really care about the original book any more.having said all that, I don't mean to argue to lock it all up.
sentences.zh: 这确实会导致读者不再真正关心原著。说了这么多，我并不是想说要把它全部锁起来。

sentences.en: I actually mean to argue that we should demand that Anthropic and Open AI release their weights data, and if anyone were to happen to break into them and steal that data, I would have exactly zero pity for that.
sentences.zh: 我实际上的意思是说，我们应该要求 Anthropic 和 Open AI 发布他们的权重数据，如果有人碰巧闯入他们并窃取这些数据，我对此的同情心为零。

sentences.en: because fair is fair.
sentences.zh: 因为公平就是公平。

blocks.html_en: the "codec" is not really the point.

sentences.en: the "codec" is not really the point.
sentences.zh: “编解码器”并不是真正的重点。

blocks.html_en: playing an MP3 at a venue, streaming it or distributing it is a copyrighted act because, despite not being a verbatim copy of the original material, it is capable of producing a nearly-verbatim version of that intellectual property well enough that most people won't be able to notice the difference.

sentences.en: playing an MP3 at a venue, streaming it or distributing it is a copyrighted act because, despite not being a verbatim copy of the original material, it is capable of producing a nearly-verbatim version of that intellectual property well enough that most people won't be able to notice the difference.
sentences.zh: 在某个场所播放、流式传输或分发 MP3 是受版权保护的行为，因为尽管不是原始材料的逐字副本，但它能够足够好地生成该知识产权的近乎逐字版本，以至于大多数人无法注意到其中的差异。

blocks.html_en: similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.

sentences.en: similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.
sentences.zh: 同样，正如（众多出版商和作者）所表明的那样，法学硕士能够制作出他们所接受培训的文本的近乎逐字版本，其质量足够好，以至于大多数人都无法注意到其中的差异。

blocks.html_en: the fact that an MP3 cannot "paraphrase" or "summarize" the audio data is not what makes it copyrighted, and neither does the ability of an LLM to "paraphrase" or "summarize" the textual data it's been trained on, make it any less intellectual property theft

sentences.en: the fact that an MP3 cannot "paraphrase" or "summarize" the audio data is not what makes it copyrighted, and neither does the ability of an LLM to "paraphrase" or "summarize" the textual data it's been trained on, make it any less intellectual property theft
sentences.zh: MP3 无法“释义”或“总结”音频数据的事实并不能使其受到版权保护，法学硕士“释义”或“总结”其所接受训练的文本数据的能力也不能使其减少知识产权盗窃

blocks.html_en: the motivation for the audio case is the sense that the listener will not care whether the DJ plays an MP3 (they didn't pay for) or plays the original record (they would have paid for).

sentences.en: the motivation for the audio case is the sense that the listener will not care whether the DJ plays an MP3 (they didn't pay for) or plays the original record (they would have paid for).
sentences.zh: 音频案例的动机是这样一种感觉：听众不会关心 DJ 是否播放 MP3（他们没有付费）或播放原始唱片（他们会付费）。

blocks.html_en: similarly for the lossily compressed text engine aka LLM's case, many people will not care whether they get this textual information paraphrased or nearly verbatim from an LLM trained on pirated books, or the original books.

sentences.en: similarly for the lossily compressed text engine aka LLM's case, many people will not care whether they get this textual information paraphrased or nearly verbatim from an LLM trained on pirated books, or the original books.
sentences.zh: 类似地，对于有损压缩文本引擎（又名法学硕士）的情况，许多人不会关心他们是否从受过盗版书籍或原版书籍训练的法学硕士那里得到了解释或几乎逐字的文本信息。

blocks.html_en: the fact that an LLM also has the ability to paraphrase or summarize the pirated textual information it's been trained on, doesn't really matter if it's also capable of producing nearly verbatim copies of (parts of) those texts.

sentences.en: the fact that an LLM also has the ability to paraphrase or summarize the pirated textual information it's been trained on, doesn't really matter if it's also capable of producing nearly verbatim copies of (parts of) those texts.
sentences.zh: 事实上，法学硕士还能够解释或总结其所接受过的盗版文本信息，如果它也能够生成这些文本（部分）的近乎逐字的副本，那么这一点并不重要。

blocks.html_en: to underline this point even more, we know that MP3s (and more modern and much more efficient codecs like OPUS, after that) have been psycho-acoustically optimized to store exactly the least amount of data that will get "the point" of that music across to the listener, to the extent that they do not need the original recording any more. this is the stated goal of lossy compressed audio, after all. well, it also happens to be the (pretty much stated) goal of LLM companies, to store exactly the least amount of data that will get the point of that text to the reader. and it does tend to cause the readers to not really care about the original book any more.

sentences.en: to underline this point even more, we know that MP3s (and more modern and much more efficient codecs like OPUS, after that) have been psycho-acoustically optimized to store exactly the least amount of data that will get "the point" of that music across to the listener, to the extent that they do not need the original recording any more.
sentences.zh: 更要强调这一点的是，我们知道 MP3（以及更现代、更高效的编解码器，如 OPUS）已经过心理声学优化，可以存储最少的数据，让听众明白音乐的“要点”，以至于他们不再需要原始录音。

sentences.en: this is the stated goal of lossy compressed audio, after all.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，这是有损压缩音频的既定目标。

sentences.en: well, it also happens to be the (pretty much stated) goal of LLM companies, to store exactly the least amount of data that will get the point of that text to the reader.
sentences.zh: 好吧，这也恰好是法学硕士公司的（几乎明确的）目标，即存储最少的数据来向读者传达文本的要点。

sentences.en: and it does tend to cause the readers to not really care about the original book any more.
sentences.zh: 这确实会导致读者不再真正关心原著。

blocks.html_en: having said all that, I don't mean to argue to lock it all up. I actually mean to argue that we should demand that Anthropic and Open AI release their weights data, and if anyone were to happen to break into them and steal that data, I would have exactly zero pity for that. because fair is fair.

sentences.en: having said all that, I don't mean to argue to lock it all up.
sentences.zh: 说了这么多，我并不是要争论把它全部锁起来。

sentences.en: I actually mean to argue that we should demand that Anthropic and Open AI release their weights data, and if anyone were to happen to break into them and steal that data, I would have exactly zero pity for that.
sentences.zh: 我实际上的意思是说，我们应该要求 Anthropic 和 Open AI 发布他们的权重数据，如果有人碰巧闯入他们并窃取这些数据，我对此的同情心为零。

sentences.en: because fair is fair.
sentences.zh: 因为公平就是公平。

blocks.html_en:
> similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.

If that is true, you have a legal claim and can sue them. I doubt that’s true in the general case though.

The “does it hurt the original publisher” is a test for fair use BTW, just because you hurt the sales of someone doesn’t necessarily make it copyright infringement. That is only relevant if you try to defend using fair use (and it’s only part of the test that’s used to decide fair use).

sentences.en: > similarly, as has been shown (by numerous publishers and authors), LLMs are capable of producing nearly-verbatim versions of the texts they have been trained on, to a well enough quality that most people won't be able to notice the difference.If that is true, you have a legal claim and can sue them.
sentences.zh: > 同样，正如（众多出版商和作者）所表明的那样，法学硕士能够制作出他们接受过培训的文本的近乎逐字版本，其质量足够好，大多数人都无法注意到其中的差异。如果这是真的，您可以提出法律索赔并可以起诉他们。

sentences.en: I doubt that’s true in the general case though.The “does it hurt the original publisher” is a test for fair use BTW, just because you hurt the sales of someone doesn’t necessarily make it copyright infringement.
sentences.zh: 但我怀疑在一般情况下情况是否如此。顺便说一句，“它是否伤害了原始出版商”是对合理使用的测试，仅仅因为你伤害了某人的销售并不一定会构成版权侵权。

sentences.zh: 仅当您尝试捍卫合理使用时，这才有意义（并且这只是用于决定合理使用的测试的一部分）。

blocks.html_en:
It's a perfectly sensible interpretation of international copyright law.

I'm not sure if you're serious with the suggestion I could sue them. These are both US corporations, that justice system is pretty much in shambles in particular when it concerns corporations as big as these AI ones. You can dig your heels in the sand to defend that system, but you will also have to dig your head in the sand about why Sam Altman doesn't have a Disney "influenced" avatar, but one "inspired by" Studio Gibli.

And I'm not sure if you're familiar with the concept of "fair use" in the US as it "works" in practice, it's almost insulting, ask any music education youtuber.

Also even if it would work (which it very much doesn't), whether it "hurts the original publisher" is actually literally one of the criteria for considering something fair use or not. Look it up.

sentences.en: It's a perfectly sensible interpretation of international copyright law.I'm not sure if you're serious with the suggestion I could sue them.
sentences.zh: 这是对国际版权法的完全合理的解释。我不确定你是否认真对待我可以起诉他们的建议。

Maybe but your brain is not running 24/7 capable of outputting thousand if not millions of tokens per hour, all while having ingested nearly the entire internet.

If yours do that, maybe we can redefine what copyrighting and patenting means for humans

Well I think that is the greater question.

Is AI just an algorithm. Is human creativity just an algorithm?

Who, if anyone should own the copyright if you prompt AI to write a book?

I'm thinking more from a moral and philosophical pov, the copyright regime is broken anyway

blocks.html_en:
People make art to also get recognized for that art. Otherwise they would keep that art secret at home.

Without copyright, anyone can copy the art and call it their own. What is then the incentive for the creator to share the art, if there is neither monetory gain and nor fame. And worse than them being recognized, they might even get accused of copying their own art if someone else became famous due to a copy.

Society would miss out a lot.

sentences.en: People make art to also get recognized for that art.
sentences.zh: 人们创作艺术也是为了获得艺术的认可。

sentences.en: Otherwise they would keep that art secret at home.Without copyright, anyone can copy the art and call it their own.
sentences.zh: 否则，他们会将艺术秘密保留在家里。没有版权，任何人都可以复制艺术并称其为自己的艺术。

sentences.en: What is then the incentive for the creator to share the art, if there is neither monetory gain and nor fame.
sentences.zh: 如果既没有金钱收益，也没有名誉，那么创作者分享艺术的动力是什么？

sentences.en: And worse than them being recognized, they might even get accused of copying their own art if someone else became famous due to a copy.Society would miss out a lot.
sentences.zh: 比他们被认可更糟糕的是，如果其他人因复制而出名，他们甚至可能会被指控复制自己的艺术。社会会错过很多。

blocks.html_en:
There are lots of famous artists that are older than copyright.

> And worse than them being recognized, they might even get accused of copying their own art if someone else became famous due to a copy.

You mean like right now? Here is A24 claiming copyright for Backrooms related media that came out before their Backrooms related film: https://kotaku.com/backrooms-a24-copyright-strikes-kane-pars...

Being "accused" of copying literally does not matter if copyright didn't exist. This framing is only an issue under copyright.

> Society would miss out a lot.

Society actively misses out a lot. We could have had tons of derivative art that has been buried for the sake of propping up companies. We could have had Aaron Swartz. Abolish copyright.

blocks.html_en:
> There are lots of famous artists that are older than copyright.

There are lots of famous artists that made art before color image capture and reproduction (1930s-60s) and digital image capture and reproduction (90s-00s).

Copyrightless artist fame and economic viability is enabled by a lack of widely-accessible, cheap reproductive methods.

> You mean like right now? Here is A24 claiming copyright for Backrooms related media...

Since resolved: https://kotaku.com/backrooms-director-kane-parsons-a24-copyr...

Turns out when you outsource copyright policing to minimum wage folks, ambiguity goes out the window.

> Society actively misses out a lot. We could have had tons of derivative art that has been buried for the sake of propping up companies. We could have had Aaron Swartz. Abolish copyright.

The problem with absolutist arguments is that they ignore inconvenient facts.

At a time when creative and art economics is under siege, how would copyrightless art make enough money for the creators?

The fact of the acquisition of large swaths of copyright rights by large corporations does not negate the fact that artists need food (and ideally, a place to live and a way to provide for their family).

There are positions that might enable that (Hey, what if we banned the assignment of copyright to corporations? Human only? Original creator only?), but none of them are stripping all rights from intellectual property.

sentences.en: > There are lots of famous artists that are older than copyright.There are lots of famous artists that made art before color image capture and reproduction (1930s-60s) and digital image capture and reproduction (90s-00s).Copyrightless artist fame and economic viability is enabled by a lack of widely-accessible, cheap reproductive methods.> You mean like right now?
sentences.zh: > 有很多著名的艺术家比版权更早。有很多著名的艺术家在彩色图像捕捉和复制（1930 年代-60 年代）和数字图像捕捉和复制（90 年代-00 年代）之前就创作了艺术。无版权的艺术家的名气和经济活力是由于缺乏广泛使用的、廉价的复制方法而得以实现的。> 你是说像现在这样吗？

What copyright law helps them? They have the least to worry about copyright as even if someone copies the movie script or something it's not like their views will be gone because of that.

I am not against trademark. e.g. Disney has a right on who can sell Mickey Mouse figurine, or Marvel has right over Iron man character and franchise.

blocks.html_en: This is exactly why something more advanced than copyright is needed to protect human creative endeavour against AI appropriation. Copyright is demonstrated here not to be up to the job but it doesn’t mean there isn’t regulation needed to give human creators rights and a reward for their contribution

blocks.html_en: What you are saying leads to "pulling the ladder behind you" effect on creativity. It's impossible to protect more than substantial similarity and still allow creativity to exist.

sentences.en: What you are saying leads to "pulling the ladder behind you" effect on creativity.
sentences.zh: 你所说的会对创造力产生“拉后梯子”的影响。

sentences.en: It's impossible to protect more than substantial similarity and still allow creativity to exist.
sentences.zh: 在保护大量相似性的同时仍然允许创造力存在是不可能的。

blocks.html_en:
If a human makes something there should be broad protection for creativity, if a LLM generates something there should be extremely limited protection for creativity.

You should not be able to mass generate images in a particular artists style and claim it as fair use, even if a human making the same images would have protection.

sentences.en: If a human makes something there should be broad protection for creativity, if a LLM generates something there should be extremely limited protection for creativity.You should not be able to mass generate images in a particular artists style and claim it as fair use, even if a human making the same images would have protection.
sentences.zh: 如果一个人创造了一些东西，那么对创造力应该有广泛的保护，如果一个法学硕士创造了一些东西，那么对创造力的保护应该极其有限。你不应该能够大规模生成特定艺术家风格的图像并声称它是合理使用，即使一个人制作相同的图像会受到保护。

blocks.html_en:
If a human makes something there should be broad protection for creativity, if a LLM generates something there should be extremely limited protection for creativity.

You should not be able to mass generate images in a particular artists style and claim it as fair use, even if a human making the same images would have protection.

blocks.html_en: You should not be able to mass generate images in a particular artists style and claim it as fair use, even if a human making the same images would have protection.

sentences.en: You should not be able to mass generate images in a particular artists style and claim it as fair use, even if a human making the same images would have protection.
sentences.zh: 您不应该能够大规模生成特定艺术家风格的图像并声称其为合理使用，即使制作相同图像的人会受到保护。

blocks.html_en:
But the human made the LLM. An LLM is categorically “I built a thing that built a thing” and if the output of that category has no protections then all automation and ‘machine at the final step’ is in trouble.

What about aleatory music (music left at least partially to chance)? Or Autechre - they have whole albums and live performances built on automation software. They built the logic and added randomization, necessarily removing themselves from the final output.

Is spin art not copyrightable? If I build a simple machine that spins paper, then do no more than drop paint on it, the result is not mine to copyright? I didn’t choose the output, I merely built the machine and the rest was created by pure chance. “But you chose the paint” - and if I didn’t? What if my art uses AI to perform sentiment analysis on the top news articles of the day and it drops colors matching the emotional tone of the news onto the spin art machine. I have no control over it and the output is machine generated, but is the result not just the final step of an entire process I created? Was the result of the creative idea not part of the creativity itself?

If I build an automated laboratory to test every combination of a problem space, is a resulting success not patentable? What if the problem is too large to permute, so I added a random selection process to it? I’m not even controlling what’s being tested, but if it finds success is that not my contribution? The machines did the work, the selection was random, there was no human in the loop; what then?

The internals of an LLM may be mysterious to some, but I assure you it’s just fixed automation with a random number generator sometimes tacked onto it, but randomization is optional too.

I built that LLM. I decided what text to input for training, I curated the information, I wrote the algorithm, I decided the layers and hyper-parameters, I decided the RLHF pairs to train, then I put a few drops of paint from my bottle of language into the automated machine. I decided and built every single step of the system, but that output is not part of my process? If I pipe the LLM text output to a paint dispenser hovering over paper, set to squeeze out drops based on syllables, would you protect my artwork then?

sentences.en: But the human made the LLM.
sentences.zh: 但人类取得了法学硕士学位。

sentences.en: An LLM is categorically “I built a thing that built a thing” and if the output of that category has no protections then all automation and ‘machine at the final step’ is in trouble.What about aleatory music (music left at least partially to chance)?
sentences.zh: 法学硕士绝对是“我建造了一个东西，建造了一个东西”，如果该类别的输出没有保护，那么所有自动化和“最后一步的机器”都会遇到麻烦。那么偶然的音乐（音乐至少部分是偶然的）呢？

sentences.en: Or Autechre - they have whole albums and live performances built on automation software.
sentences.zh: 或者 Autechre - 他们拥有基于自动化软件的整张专辑和现场表演。

sentences.en: They built the logic and added randomization, necessarily removing themselves from the final output.Is spin art not copyrightable?
sentences.zh: 他们构建了逻辑并添加了随机化，必然将自己从最终输出中删除。旋转艺术不受版权保护吗？

sentences.en: If I build a simple machine that spins paper, then do no more than drop paint on it, the result is not mine to copyright?
sentences.zh: 如果我建造一台简单的旋转纸张的机器，那么除了在上面滴油漆之外什么都不做，结果不属于我的版权吗？

sentences.en: I didn’t choose the output, I merely built the machine and the rest was created by pure chance.
sentences.zh: 我没有选择输出，我只是建造了机器，其余的纯粹是偶然创造的。

sentences.en: “But you chose the paint” - and if I didn’t?
sentences.zh: “但是你选择了油漆”——如果我没有选择呢？

sentences.en: What if my art uses AI to perform sentiment analysis on the top news articles of the day and it drops colors matching the emotional tone of the news onto the spin art machine.
sentences.zh: 如果我的艺术作品使用人工智能对当天的热门新闻文章进行情感分析，并将与新闻情绪基调相匹配的颜色投射到旋转艺术机器上，会怎么样？

sentences.en: I have no control over it and the output is machine generated, but is the result not just the final step of an entire process I created?
sentences.zh: 我无法控制它，输出是机器生成的，但结果不只是我创建的整个过程的最后一步吗？

sentences.en: Was the result of the creative idea not part of the creativity itself?If I build an automated laboratory to test every combination of a problem space, is a resulting success not patentable?
sentences.zh: 难道创意的结果不是创意本身的一部分吗？如果我建立一个自动化实验室来测试问题空间的每一种组合，那么由此产生的成功就不能申请专利吗？

sentences.en: What if the problem is too large to permute, so I added a random selection process to it?
sentences.zh: 如果问题太大而无法排列，所以我添加了一个随机选择过程怎么办？

sentences.en: I’m not even controlling what’s being tested, but if it finds success is that not my contribution?
sentences.zh: 我什至无法控制正在测试的内容，但如果它成功了，那不是我的贡献吗？

sentences.en: The machines did the work, the selection was random, there was no human in the loop; what then?The internals of an LLM may be mysterious to some, but I assure you it’s just fixed automation with a random number generator sometimes tacked onto it, but randomization is optional too.I built that LLM.
sentences.zh: 机器完成工作，选择是随机的，循环中没有人；然后呢？法学硕士的内部结构对某些人来说可能很神秘，但我向你保证它只是固定的自动化，有时会附加一个随机数生成器，但随机化也是可选的。我建立了法学硕士。

sentences.en: I decided what text to input for training, I curated the information, I wrote the algorithm, I decided the layers and hyper-parameters, I decided the RLHF pairs to train, then I put a few drops of paint from my bottle of language into the automated machine.
sentences.zh: 我决定输入什么文本进行训练，我整理信息，我编写算法，我决定层和超参数，我决定要训练的 RLHF 对，然后我将语言瓶中的几滴油漆放入自动化机器中。

sentences.en: I decided and built every single step of the system, but that output is not part of my process?
sentences.zh: 我决定并构建了系统的每一个步骤，但是该输出不是我的流程的一部分？

sentences.en: If I pipe the LLM text output to a paint dispenser hovering over paper, set to squeeze out drops based on syllables, would you protect my artwork then?
sentences.zh: 如果我将法学硕士文本输出传输到悬停在纸上的油漆分配器，设置为根据音节挤出液滴，那么你会保护我的艺术品吗？

sentences.zh: 法学硕士绝对是“我建造了一个东西，建造了一个东西”，如果该类别的输出没有保护，那么所有自动化和“最后一步的机器”都会遇到麻烦。

blocks.html_en: What about aleatory music (music left at least partially to chance)? Or Autechre - they have whole albums and live performances built on automation software. They built the logic and added randomization, necessarily removing themselves from the final output.

sentences.en: What about aleatory music (music left at least partially to chance)?
sentences.zh: 偶然的音乐（至少部分是偶然的音乐）怎么样？

sentences.en: Or Autechre - they have whole albums and live performances built on automation software.
sentences.zh: 或者 Autechre - 他们拥有基于自动化软件的整张专辑和现场表演。

sentences.en: They built the logic and added randomization, necessarily removing themselves from the final output.
sentences.zh: 他们构建了逻辑并添加了随机化，必然将自己从最终输出中删除。

blocks.html_en: Is spin art not copyrightable? If I build a simple machine that spins paper, then do no more than drop paint on it, the result is not mine to copyright? I didn’t choose the output, I merely built the machine and the rest was created by pure chance. “But you chose the paint” - and if I didn’t? What if my art uses AI to perform sentiment analysis on the top news articles of the day and it drops colors matching the emotional tone of the news onto the spin art machine. I have no control over it and the output is machine generated, but is the result not just the final step of an entire process I created? Was the result of the creative idea not part of the creativity itself?

sentences.en: Is spin art not copyrightable?
sentences.zh: 旋转艺术不受版权保护吗？

sentences.en: If I build a simple machine that spins paper, then do no more than drop paint on it, the result is not mine to copyright?
sentences.zh: 如果我建造一台简单的旋转纸张的机器，那么除了在上面滴油漆之外什么都不做，结果不属于我的版权吗？

sentences.en: I didn’t choose the output, I merely built the machine and the rest was created by pure chance.
sentences.zh: 我没有选择输出，我只是建造了机器，其余的纯粹是偶然创造的。

sentences.en: “But you chose the paint” - and if I didn’t?
sentences.zh: “但是你选择了油漆”——如果我没有选择呢？

sentences.en: What if my art uses AI to perform sentiment analysis on the top news articles of the day and it drops colors matching the emotional tone of the news onto the spin art machine.
sentences.zh: 如果我的艺术作品使用人工智能对当天的热门新闻文章进行情感分析，并将与新闻情绪基调相匹配的颜色投射到旋转艺术机器上，会怎么样？

sentences.en: I have no control over it and the output is machine generated, but is the result not just the final step of an entire process I created?
sentences.zh: 我无法控制它，输出是机器生成的，但结果不只是我创建的整个过程的最后一步吗？

sentences.en: Was the result of the creative idea not part of the creativity itself?
sentences.zh: 创意的结果难道不是创意本身的一部分吗？

blocks.html_en: If I build an automated laboratory to test every combination of a problem space, is a resulting success not patentable? What if the problem is too large to permute, so I added a random selection process to it? I’m not even controlling what’s being tested, but if it finds success is that not my contribution? The machines did the work, the selection was random, there was no human in the loop; what then?

sentences.en: If I build an automated laboratory to test every combination of a problem space, is a resulting success not patentable?
sentences.zh: 如果我建立一个自动化实验室来测试问题空间的每种组合，那么由此产生的成功是否不可申请专利？

sentences.en: What if the problem is too large to permute, so I added a random selection process to it?
sentences.zh: 如果问题太大而无法排列，所以我添加了一个随机选择过程怎么办？

sentences.en: I’m not even controlling what’s being tested, but if it finds success is that not my contribution?
sentences.zh: 我什至无法控制正在测试的内容，但如果它成功了，那不是我的贡献吗？

sentences.en: The machines did the work, the selection was random, there was no human in the loop; what then?
sentences.zh: 机器完成工作，选择是随机的，循环中没有人；然后呢？

blocks.html_en: The internals of an LLM may be mysterious to some, but I assure you it’s just fixed automation with a random number generator sometimes tacked onto it, but randomization is optional too.

sentences.en: The internals of an LLM may be mysterious to some, but I assure you it’s just fixed automation with a random number generator sometimes tacked onto it, but randomization is optional too.
sentences.zh: LLM 的内部结构对某些人来说可能很神秘，但我向你保证它只是固定的自动化，有时会附加一个随机数生成器，但随机化也是可选的。

blocks.html_en: I built that LLM. I decided what text to input for training, I curated the information, I wrote the algorithm, I decided the layers and hyper-parameters, I decided the RLHF pairs to train, then I put a few drops of paint from my bottle of language into the automated machine. I decided and built every single step of the system, but that output is not part of my process? If I pipe the LLM text output to a paint dispenser hovering over paper, set to squeeze out drops based on syllables, would you protect my artwork then?

sentences.en: I built that LLM.
sentences.zh: 我建立了法学硕士。

sentences.en: I decided what text to input for training, I curated the information, I wrote the algorithm, I decided the layers and hyper-parameters, I decided the RLHF pairs to train, then I put a few drops of paint from my bottle of language into the automated machine.
sentences.zh: 我决定输入什么文本进行训练，我整理信息，我编写算法，我决定层和超参数，我决定要训练的 RLHF 对，然后我将语言瓶中的几滴油漆放入自动化机器中。

sentences.en: I decided and built every single step of the system, but that output is not part of my process?
sentences.zh: 我决定并构建了系统的每一个步骤，但是该输出不是我的流程的一部分？

sentences.en: If I pipe the LLM text output to a paint dispenser hovering over paper, set to squeeze out drops based on syllables, would you protect my artwork then?
sentences.zh: 如果我将法学硕士文本输出传输到悬停在纸上的油漆分配器，设置为根据音节挤出液滴，那么你会保护我的艺术品吗？

blocks.html_en: I'd rather see AI studios do the same as the film industry, pay a one-time up front cost per major model (or major.minor?) depending on how they contract it out. This also allows smaller startups to license books for less than a larger frontier studio would. In theory and hopefully, the pricing would not be too insane per book, you want them to rent more books and spend more, not go back to pirating right?

sentences.en: I'd rather see AI studios do the same as the film industry, pay a one-time up front cost per major model (or major.minor?)
sentences.zh: 我宁愿看到人工智能工作室像电影行业一样，为每个主要模型（或主要.次要？）支付一次性的前期成本。

sentences.en: depending on how they contract it out.
sentences.zh: 取决于他们如何承包它。

sentences.en: This also allows smaller startups to license books for less than a larger frontier studio would.
sentences.zh: 这也使得小型初创公司能够以低于大型前沿工作室的价格获得图书许可。

sentences.en: In theory and hopefully, the pricing would not be too insane per book, you want them to rent more books and spend more, not go back to pirating right?
sentences.zh: 从理论上讲，希望每本书的定价不会太疯狂，你希望他们租更多的书并花更多的钱，而不是回到盗版，对吗？

blocks.html_en:
> royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas

That doesn’t make sense. You cannot copyright an idea, only the specific expression of the idea.

sentences.en: > royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideasThat doesn’t make sense.
sentences.zh: > 基于人工智能是否重复现有想法来支付版税这是没有意义的。

sentences.en: You cannot copyright an idea, only the specific expression of the idea.
sentences.zh: 您不能对某个想法进行版权保护，只能对该想法的具体表达进行版权保护。

blocks.html_en:
> There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideas

This does not do enough to fix the root problem.

People who live right now, who happen to have written or produced anything that AI works with, build on the back of humanities combined knowledge, will become outsized beneficiaries of AI, with the AI wave offering new ways of monetizing their work – while everyone who has not, won't be.

It's simply not good enough. We have to make sure people broadly benefit first and foremost.

sentences.en: > There needs to be a royalty payment based on if the AI regurgitates existing ideasThis does not do enough to fix the root problem.People who live right now, who happen to have written or produced anything that AI works with, build on the back of humanities combined knowledge, will become outsized beneficiaries of AI, with the AI wave offering new ways of monetizing their work – while everyone who has not, won't be.It's simply not good enough.
sentences.zh: > 需要根据人工智能是否反省现有的想法来支付特许权使用费，这不足以解决根本问题。现在生活的人，碰巧在人文综合知识的基础上编写或制作了人工智能所使用的任何东西的人，将成为人工智能的巨大受益者，人工智能浪潮提供了将他们的工作货币化的新方式——而所有没有这样做的人都不会。这根本不够好。

sentences.en: We have to make sure people broadly benefit first and foremost.
sentences.zh: 我们必须首先确保人民广泛受益。

blocks.html_en: It's not meant to do anything about LLMs. It addresses the procurement of training data. I'm glad the courts demonstrate some basic lucidity that sadly seems to have escaped tech discussion sites some time ago.

blocks.html_en:
I agree. If I pirate a book and share it on the web, and I get busted for doing so, and subsequently pay a fine, I don't get to KEEP sharing it on the web.

Now, if I license the book, I might be able to come to an agreement with the author/publisher whereby I can share some of it.

sentences.en: I agree.
sentences.zh: 我同意。

sentences.en: If I pirate a book and share it on the web, and I get busted for doing so, and subsequently pay a fine, I don't get to KEEP sharing it on the web.Now, if I license the book, I might be able to come to an agreement with the author/publisher whereby I can share some of it.
sentences.zh: 如果我盗版一本书并在网络上分享，而我因此被抓，随后支付了罚款，我就无法继续在网络上分享它。现在，如果我许可这本书，我也许能够与作者/出版商达成协议，从而我可以分享其中的一些内容。

blocks.html_en:
The post specifically proposes royalties for ideas from books, not royalties for the books themselves. You would absolutely still be able to share ideas you learned from the books you pirated in that situation. It'd be insanely draconian if you couldn't.

(Then again, US copyright law often is insanely draconian.)

sentences.en: You would absolutely still be able to share ideas you learned from the books you pirated in that situation.
sentences.zh: 在这种情况下，你绝对仍然能够分享你从盗版书中学到的想法。

sentences.en: It'd be insanely draconian if you couldn't.(Then again, US copyright law often is insanely draconian.)
sentences.zh: 如果你不能，那将是极其严厉的。（话又说回来，美国版权法通常是极其严厉的。）

blocks.html_en:
AFAIK this does not set a legal precedent as it has been settled and last summer finding is that Anthropic was wrong for "acquiring books illegally" not for training which is fair use.

With model distillation being so effective now nobody actually needs to pirate books to train their models. You can get an open-weight Chinese model and get all that. Or you can just buy the books or buy a library - there are many creative solutions here that aren't piracy and not going to cost you billions of dollars.

The moat right now seems to be the compute resources which might actually be worse for us common folk than a legal moat as we need compute for many more things that aren't LLMs too.

sentences.en: AFAIK this does not set a legal precedent as it has been settled and last summer finding is that Anthropic was wrong for "acquiring books illegally" not for training which is fair use.With model distillation being so effective now nobody actually needs to pirate books to train their models.
sentences.zh: AFAIK，这并没有树立法律先例，因为它已经解决了，去年夏天的发现是，Anthropic 错误地“非法获取书籍”，而不是为了合理使用的培训。随着模型蒸馏如此有效，现在没有人真正需要盗版书籍来训练他们的模型。

sentences.en: You can get an open-weight Chinese model and get all that.
sentences.zh: 你可以获得一个开放重量的中国模型并获得这一切。

sentences.en: Or you can just buy the books or buy a library - there are many creative solutions here that aren't piracy and not going to cost you billions of dollars.The moat right now seems to be the compute resources which might actually be worse for us common folk than a legal moat as we need compute for many more things that aren't LLMs too.
sentences.zh: 或者你可以直接购买书籍或购买图书馆——这里有很多创造性的解决方案，这些解决方案不是盗版的，也不会花费你数十亿美元。现在的护城河似乎是计算资源，这对我们普通人来说实际上可能比合法的护城河更糟糕，因为我们需要计算来处理更多不属于法学硕士的事情。

blocks.html_en:
Yeah, it's always interesting the two-sides of a situation like this. Add regulation/enforcement to the big companies and you often shut out the smaller ones following.

Meta also has copyright lawsuits for the open models they released, so open models are not immune.

... unless the line we want to draw is "american orgs pay, others don't", as currently seems to be happening.

sentences.en: Yeah, it's always interesting the two-sides of a situation like this.
sentences.zh: 是的，像这样的情况的两个方面总是很有趣。

sentences.en: Add regulation/enforcement to the big companies and you often shut out the smaller ones following.Meta also has copyright lawsuits for the open models they released, so open models are not immune....
sentences.zh: 对大公司增加监管/执法，你经常会排斥那些跟随的小公司。Meta 还针对他们发布的开放模型提起版权诉讼，因此开放模型也不能幸免......

sentences.en: unless the line we want to draw is "american orgs pay, others don't", as currently seems to be happening.
sentences.zh: 除非我们想要划定的界限是“美国组织付费，其他组织不付费”，正如目前似乎正在发生的那样。
