blocks.html_en:
> Add regulation/enforcement to the big companies and you often shut out the smaller ones following.

That is the case, any regulation increases the cost to enter a market.

But in this case, its irrelevant because the moat of cost to enter is already unfathomable and secondly, they are not adding regulation but fining them for committing a crime.

So yeah, adding that every food compnay needs 3 health inspectors that they pay for would benefit coca cola over you mom and pop bakery. But telling someone they cannot start a Space agency with money laundered from ransom and drug sales payments would not affect much the competition markets

sentences.en: > Add regulation/enforcement to the big companies and you often shut out the smaller ones following.That is the case, any regulation increases the cost to enter a market.But in this case, its irrelevant because the moat of cost to enter is already unfathomable and secondly, they are not adding regulation but fining them for committing a crime.So yeah, adding that every food compnay needs 3 health inspectors that they pay for would benefit coca cola over you mom and pop bakery.
sentences.zh: > 对大公司增加监管/执法，你经常将较小的公司拒之门外。在这种情况下，任何监管都会增加进入市场的成本。但在这种情况下，它无关紧要，因为进入成本的护城河已经深不可测，其次，他们不是增加监管，而是对犯罪的人进行罚款。所以，是的，补充说每个食品公司都需要 3 名他们支付费用的卫生检查员，这将使可口可乐比你的妈妈和流行面包店受益。

blocks.html_en:
There are multiple ways to respond to that and I will try and summarise them.

Current believe is that its a "winner takes all market", so companies are acting rationally and using Brute Force compute to get there first. Training costs scale linearly, which means the moat is directly related to compute cost

There are theories that they are wasting 90% of training costs and there are more efficient ways to do it than throw compute at the problem. But if thats the case then chances are the market is not "winner takes all". Which then means the valuation of the ENTIRE market is overvalued.

Basically the only way for the assertion "at the moment" to be true is if the market is a bubble, else if the current theory of winner takes all market means a monopoly will make it so that cost isnt even the worst of the moats to enter.

sentences.en: There are multiple ways to respond to that and I will try and summarise them.Current believe is that its a "winner takes all market", so companies are acting rationally and using Brute Force compute to get there first.
sentences.zh: 对此有多种方法可以应对，我将尝试对它们进行总结。目前认为这是一个“赢者通吃的市场”，因此公司正在理性行动并使用强力计算来首先实现这一目标。

sentences.en: Training costs scale linearly, which means the moat is directly related to compute costThere are theories that they are wasting 90% of training costs and there are more efficient ways to do it than throw compute at the problem.
sentences.zh: 训练成本呈线性增长，这意味着护城河与计算成本直接相关。有理论认为，他们浪费了 90% 的训练成本，并且有比用计算来解决问题更有效的方法。

sentences.en: But if thats the case then chances are the market is not "winner takes all".
sentences.zh: 但如果真是这样，那么市场很可能就不是“赢者通吃”。

sentences.en: Which then means the valuation of the ENTIRE market is overvalued.Basically the only way for the assertion "at the moment" to be true is if the market is a bubble, else if the current theory of winner takes all market means a monopoly will make it so that cost isnt even the worst of the moats to enter.
sentences.zh: 这意味着整个市场的估值被高估了。基本上，“目前”这一断言成立的唯一方法是市场是否存在泡沫，否则，如果当前的赢家通吃的理论意味着垄断将实现这一目标，因此成本甚至不是最糟糕的护城河。

blocks.html_en: I think the only way to stop that is to put the responsible folks in prison permanently. Small criminals are being jailed permanently on repeated offence. A leech system for lawyers and judges.

blocks.html_en: It's easy to beat up on OpenAI and Anthropic, because they have lots of money and knowingly broke the law, but writing the book was onetime work too. Do we really want to turn everything into recurring revenue stream to skim of? How would that even work for an open weights model? Would you say the same about a human educating themselves from a book? The answer has to be more than pearl clutching for poor starving little authors (and the not so poor class action lawyers).

sentences.en: It's easy to beat up on OpenAI and Anthropic, because they have lots of money and knowingly broke the law, but writing the book was onetime work too.

sentences.en: Do we really want to turn everything into recurring revenue stream to skim of?
sentences.zh: 我们真的想把一切都变成可以掠夺的经常性收入流吗？

sentences.en: How would that even work for an open weights model?
sentences.zh: 对于开放权重模型来说，这又如何呢？

sentences.en: Would you say the same about a human educating themselves from a book?
sentences.zh: 对于一个人通过书本进行自我教育，你会这么说吗？

blocks.html_en:
If you have the time, read the judge's response to the motion:

https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cand.43...

The big deal for publishers and authors is the payout per eligible title is $3k. For a traditional publishing contract involving one author, the amount will be split down the middle.

The other thing which caught my eye is the judge slashed the class counsel's fee by half, from 12.5% ($187.5m) to 6.8% ($101m). The class counsel's unreimbursed litigation expenses were $2.6m.

The three class representatives get just $15k each.

If you have the time, read the judge's response to the motion:

https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cand.43...

The big deal for publishers and authors is the payout per eligible title is $3k. For a traditional publishing contract involving one author, the amount will be split down the middle.

The other thing which caught my eye is the judge slashed the class counsel's fee by half, from 12.5% ($187.5m) to 6.8% ($101m). The class counsel's unreimbursed litigation expenses were $2.6m.

The three class representatives get just $15k each.

blocks.html_en: Realtors are capped in the percentage they can take for selling properties. Brokers and financial advisers are capped in their fees. My presumption is that the only reason this very standard and reasonable regulatory pattern doesn’t affect lawyers is because they tend to be the ones writing and enforcing the regulations in the first place.

sentences.en: Realtors are capped in the percentage they can take for selling properties.
sentences.zh: 房地产经纪人出售房产的佣金比例受到限制。

sentences.en: Brokers and financial advisers are capped in their fees.
sentences.zh: 经纪人和财务顾问的费用有上限。

sentences.en: My presumption is that the only reason this very standard and reasonable regulatory pattern doesn’t affect lawyers is because they tend to be the ones writing and enforcing the regulations in the first place.
sentences.zh: 我的推测是，这种非常标准和合理的监管模式不会影响律师的唯一原因是，他们往往是首先制定和执行法规的人。

blocks.html_en:
> pattern doesn’t affect lawyers is because they tend to be the ones writing ... the regulations in the first place

In my state, the people hired to write new bills (if passed becoming statute) have to pass their JD (law degree). Legislators only get to request new bills, they can't hand a proposal (which may have been written by a lobbying agency like ALEC or Heritage) into the system.

sentences.zh: > 模式不会影响律师，因为他们往往是撰写......

sentences.zh: 首先是法规在我的州，受雇撰写新法案（如果通过成为法规）的人必须通过他们的 JD（法律学位）。

sentences.en: Legislators only get to request new bills, they can't hand a proposal (which may have been written by a lobbying agency like ALEC or Heritage) into the system.
sentences.zh: 立法者只能要求新的法案，他们不能将提案（可能是由 ALEC 或 Heritage 等游说机构编写的）提交到系统中。

blocks.html_en:
There is no legal maximum for the percentage real estate agents can take, in the US. Rates are also not fixed, by law, and are required to be negotiable. There's a general standard for rates (typically 5-6%, split between agents/brokers), but there's nothing stopping them from setting it to 99%, other than the fact that people won't pay it.

Source: was a licensed real estate agent for a long time.

sentences.en: There is no legal maximum for the percentage real estate agents can take, in the US.
sentences.zh: 在美国，房地产经纪人可以收取的百分比没有法定上限。

sentences.en: Rates are also not fixed, by law, and are required to be negotiable.
sentences.zh: 根据法律，费率也不是固定的，并且需要可以协商。

blocks.html_en:
No. "Realtor" is a trademarked term for a member of the National Association of Realtors. Real estate agents are licensed by state governments, but prices for real estate agents are not legislated by state governments.

If this is the 2024 settlement that you are referring to, it did not say anything about the price a Realtor can charge:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burnett_v._National_Associatio...

>The cooperative compensation rule has been eliminated as a result of the settlement. Seller's agents are no longer required to offer compensation to buyer's agents when listing a home for sale on a Realtor-owned multiple listing service. In addition, Realtors acting as buyer's agents must enter into contracts with buyers before touring any homes, allowing buyers to negotiate how much they will pay their buyer's agent.

sentences.en: No. "Realtor" is a trademarked term for a member of the National Association of Realtors.
sentences.zh: 不。“房地产经纪人”是全国房地产经纪人协会成员的商标术语。

sentences.en: Real estate agents are licensed by state governments, but prices for real estate agents are not legislated by state governments.If this is the 2024 settlement that you are referring to, it did not say anything about the price a Realtor can charge:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burnett_v._National_Associatio...>The cooperative compensation rule has been eliminated as a result of the settlement.
sentences.zh: 房地产经纪人获得州政府许可，但房地产经纪人的价格不是由州政府立法的。如果这是您所指的 2024 年和解，它没有说明房地产经纪人可以收取的价格：https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burnett_v._National_Associatio...> 和解后，合作补偿规则已被取消。

sentences.en: Seller's agents are no longer required to offer compensation to buyer's agents when listing a home for sale on a Realtor-owned multiple listing service.
sentences.zh: 在房地产经纪人拥有的多重挂牌服务上列出待售房屋时，卖方代理人不再需要向买方代理人提供补偿。

sentences.en: In addition, Realtors acting as buyer's agents must enter into contracts with buyers before touring any homes, allowing buyers to negotiate how much they will pay their buyer's agent.
sentences.zh: 此外，作为买方代理人的房地产经纪人必须在参观任何房屋之前与买方签订合同，允许买方协商向买方代理人支付的费用。

blocks.html_en:
He wasn’t just some famous redditor. Aaron Swartz helped create Reddit and invented RSS.

If I put my conspiracy theory hat one and I always get piled on for this theory in other online communities but I think it could be possible. The theory is I think Aaron found some very dark stuff while exploring the MIT private networks, things that he was not supposed to see and could be very damaging to a lot people if they were exposed. The infamous Jeffery Epstein was donating a lot of money to MIT and its Media Labs. I think there is a much deeper story at play that the mainstream narrative is hiding with a “suicide”.

sentences.en: He wasn’t just some famous redditor.
sentences.zh: 他不仅仅是某个著名的 Reddit 用户。

sentences.en: Aaron Swartz helped create Reddit and invented RSS.If I put my conspiracy theory hat one and I always get piled on for this theory in other online communities but I think it could be possible.
sentences.zh: Aaron Swartz 帮助创建了 Reddit 并发明了 RSS。如果我把我的阴谋论戴上一顶，我总是会在其他在线社区中接受这一理论，但我认为这是可能的。

sentences.en: The theory is I think Aaron found some very dark stuff while exploring the MIT private networks, things that he was not supposed to see and could be very damaging to a lot people if they were exposed.
sentences.zh: 我的理论是，我认为亚伦在探索麻省理工学院的私人网络时发现了一些非常黑暗的东西，这些东西是他不应该看到的，如果被暴露，可能会对很多人造成很大的伤害。

sentences.en: The infamous Jeffery Epstein was donating a lot of money to MIT and its Media Labs.
sentences.zh: 臭名昭著的杰弗里·爱泼斯坦向麻省理工学院及其媒体实验室捐赠了大量资金。

sentences.en: I think there is a much deeper story at play that the mainstream narrative is hiding with a “suicide”.
sentences.zh: 我认为主流叙事中隐藏着一个更深层次的故事：“自杀”。

If he found fucked up stuff regarding crimes way worse than his on university networks don't you think he could have gotten out of the sentencing entirely by cooperating against them

Epstein was relatively restrained even in his personal email, I doubt he was using MIT administrated systems to facilitate a pedo ring

blocks.html_en: It's not that simple. If he did find dark stuff, and he got caught - he will have both the state murderers and the filth on his case. That's the best case scenario assuming the state isn't on the side of the filth by which case he was cooked no matter what he did. Filth does not even have come after you. They can just send you a family portrait and you'd know the hole is deep and only one way out.

blocks.html_en:
I'm not from the USA so my views are obviously biased by this. I very much doubt Epstein committed suicide, and it's been wild to watch you guys deal with it, not releasing the files, holding nobody accountable and so on. That being said, I also don't think there needs to be a big dark secret to explain why someone caught in the USA justice system would commit suicide.

It's crazy that you could face 35 years in jail for trying to free knowledge in a harmless manner. The longest anyone has been imprisoned for in my country in modern times is 26 years, 11 months and 6 days. We have a few people posed to break that record. Peter Lundin has been in prison for 25ish years, and him and Peter Madsen (the discount elon musk turned murderer who killed some poor journalist in is selfmade submarine) are contenders to people who will probably go beyond 35 years.

Not that our system is perfect. I think we're far too lenient on some crimes, but risking 35 years in jail for downloading and sharing academic knowledge... That's objectively evil.

blocks.html_en:
So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?

It's funny that crimes can be settled in cash. IOW, everything has a price; and the price is always right. Settlement ought to be the euphemism for blood money.

In addition to the settlement, what I'd consider fair is to have these companies pay royalties in perpetuity. Of course, that's not tractable.

sentences.en: So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?It's funny that crimes can be settled in cash.
sentences.zh: 那么，在未经他人事先同意的情况下，继续从他人的工作中大规模获利——永远——是合理使用吗？有趣的是，犯罪行为可以用现金解决。

sentences.en: IOW, everything has a price; and the price is always right.
sentences.zh: IOW，一切皆有代价；而且价格总是合适的。

sentences.en: Settlement ought to be the euphemism for blood money.In addition to the settlement, what I'd consider fair is to have these companies pay royalties in perpetuity.
sentences.zh: 和解应该是血汗钱的委婉说法。除了和解之外，我认为公平的是让这些公司永久支付专利费。

sentences.en: Of course, that's not tractable.
sentences.zh: 当然，这并不容易处理。

blocks.html_en:
> So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?

No, that's what they got in trouble for - a lack of consent.

If the author consents, it would have been fine. If they bought the books, then it is fine. Digitisation through destruction, like most book scanning systems. As long as the original work is destroyed during the process, and you actually paid for it, then it is fair use.

If it regurgitates, then the author can sue you again. So you are incentivised to make damn sure it doesn't. That's not covered by fair use.

Its only if the original cannot be accessed anymore, and you paid to get the original. Both must be true, for fair use to hold.

sentences.en: > So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?No, that's what they got in trouble for - a lack of consent.If the author consents, it would have been fine.
sentences.zh: > 那么，在未经他人事先同意的情况下，继续从他人的作品中大规模获利，是否属于合理使用？不，这就是他们陷入麻烦的原因——缺乏同意。如果作者同意，那就没问题了。

sentences.en: If they bought the books, then it is fine.
sentences.zh: 如果他们买了书，那就没问题了。

sentences.en: Digitisation through destruction, like most book scanning systems.
sentences.zh: 像大多数书籍扫描系统一样，通过破坏实现数字化。

sentences.en: As long as the original work is destroyed during the process, and you actually paid for it, then it is fair use.If it regurgitates, then the author can sue you again.
sentences.zh: 只要在这个过程中原作被毁掉了，而且你确实付费了，那么它就是合理使用。如果它反流了，那么作者可以再次起诉你。

sentences.en: So you are incentivised to make damn sure it doesn't.
sentences.zh: 所以你有动力去确保它不会。

sentences.en: That's not covered by fair use.Its only if the original cannot be accessed anymore, and you paid to get the original.
sentences.zh: 这不属于合理使用范围。只有在无法再访问原件并且您付费才能获得原件的情况下才可以。

sentences.en: Both must be true, for fair use to hold.
sentences.zh: 两者都必须真实，才能合理使用。

blocks.html_en:
Format shifting has a DMCA carveout. It is 100% allowed. Since around 2000, the rule has permitted it for:

> Literary works, including computer programs and databases, protected by access control mechanisms that fail to permit access because of malfunction, damage, or obsoleteness.

DRM being covered under other laws, and being gross, still applies. And still applies to industry giants, too. Which is why most who do this, like Google, actually buy physical copies and scan it destructively, so they don't have to deal with it.

sentences.en: Format shifting has a DMCA carveout.
sentences.zh: 格式转换具有 DMCA 条款。

sentences.en: It is 100% allowed.
sentences.zh: 这是100%允许的。

sentences.en: Since around 2000, the rule has permitted it for:> Literary works, including computer programs and databases, protected by access control mechanisms that fail to permit access because of malfunction, damage, or obsoleteness.DRM being covered under other laws, and being gross, still applies.
sentences.zh: 自 2000 年左右以来，该规则允许其用于：> 受访问控制机制保护的文学作品，包括计算机程序和数据库，但由于故障、损坏或过时而不允许访问。DRM 受到其他法律的保护，尽管很粗暴，但仍然适用。

sentences.en: And still applies to industry giants, too.
sentences.zh: 并且仍然适用于行业巨头。

sentences.en: Which is why most who do this, like Google, actually buy physical copies and scan it destructively, so they don't have to deal with it.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么大多数这样做的人，比如谷歌，实际上购买了实体副本并进行破坏性扫描，这样他们就不必处​​理它。

sentences.zh: 格式转换具有 DMCA 条款。

sentences.en: It is 100% allowed.
sentences.zh: 这是100%允许的。

sentences.en: Since around 2000, the rule has permitted it for:
sentences.zh: 自 2000 年左右以来，该规则允许其用于：

blocks.html_en: > Literary works, including computer programs and databases, protected by access control mechanisms that fail to permit access because of malfunction, damage, or obsoleteness.

sentences.en: > Literary works, including computer programs and databases, protected by access control mechanisms that fail to permit access because of malfunction, damage, or obsoleteness.
sentences.zh: > 文学作品，包括计算机程序和数据库，受访问控制机制保护，但由于故障、损坏或过时而不允许访问。

blocks.html_en: DRM being covered under other laws, and being gross, still applies. And still applies to industry giants, too. Which is why most who do this, like Google, actually buy physical copies and scan it destructively, so they don't have to deal with it.

sentences.en: DRM being covered under other laws, and being gross, still applies.
sentences.zh: DRM 受到其他法律的保护，虽然很严重，但仍然适用。

sentences.en: And still applies to industry giants, too.
sentences.zh: 并且仍然适用于行业巨头。

sentences.en: Which is why most who do this, like Google, actually buy physical copies and scan it destructively, so they don't have to deal with it.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么大多数这样做的人，比如谷歌，实际上购买了实体副本并进行破坏性扫描，这样他们就不必处​​理它。

blocks.html_en:
Yes, it's legal to format-shift DRM protected media, but it is not lawful. Someone has to break the law to provide me with a decryption tool.

If you asked the right politician when all these rules were being written, the intent was that each person who needs to format-shift their media would independently write their own decryption tools, use them for lawful purposes only, and then dutifully delete them the moment they were no longer needed. This is, of course, laughable.

Of course, if Anthropic was, say, buying and decrypting Kindle books TODAY; they probably could get Claude to vibe-code a DRM decryption tool[0]. That would actually be within the bounds of this asinine law. If Anthropic started off by doing this, however, they probably would have just used a decryption tool found on the Internet, and that would have invited different legal challenges. Like, is it legal to use an unlawful tool to accomplish something otherwise legally protected? The courts so far have been very hostile to ANY attempt to tie the anticircumvention provisions of the DMCA to fair use. They could easily say "No, you only get to format shift with your own tools".

[0] Related note: I really wish I had Mythos access, just so I could jailbreak my iPad on modern iPadOS. No other reason.

sentences.en: Yes, it's legal to format-shift DRM protected media, but it is not lawful.
sentences.zh: 是的，对受 DRM 保护的媒体进行格式转换是合法的，但这是不合法的。

sentences.en: Someone has to break the law to provide me with a decryption tool.If you asked the right politician when all these rules were being written, the intent was that each person who needs to format-shift their media would independently write their own decryption tools, use them for lawful purposes only, and then dutifully delete them the moment they were no longer needed.
sentences.zh: 必须有人违反法律才能为我提供解密工具。如果你问正确的政治家何时编写所有这些规则，其目的是每个需要转换媒体格式的人都将独立编写自己的解密工具，仅将它们用于合法目的，然后在不再需要它们时尽职尽责地删除它们。

sentences.en: This is, of course, laughable.Of course, if Anthropic was, say, buying and decrypting Kindle books TODAY; they probably could get Claude to vibe-code a DRM decryption tool[0].
sentences.zh: 这当然是可笑的。当然，如果 Anthropic 今天购买并解密 Kindle 书籍的话；他们可能可以让 Claude 编写一个 DRM 解密工具 [0]。

sentences.en: That would actually be within the bounds of this asinine law.
sentences.zh: 这实际上是在这个愚蠢的法律的范围内。

sentences.en: If Anthropic started off by doing this, however, they probably would have just used a decryption tool found on the Internet, and that would have invited different legal challenges.
sentences.zh: 然而，如果 Anthropic 一开始就这样做，他们可能只会使用互联网上找到的解密工具，这会带来不同的法律挑战。

sentences.en: Like, is it legal to use an unlawful tool to accomplish something otherwise legally protected?
sentences.zh: 例如，使用非法工具来完成受法律保护的事情是否合法？

sentences.en: The courts so far have been very hostile to ANY attempt to tie the anticircumvention provisions of the DMCA to fair use.
sentences.zh: 迄今为止，法院一直非常反对任何将 DMCA 的反规避条款与合理使用联系起来的尝试。

sentences.en: They could easily say "No, you only get to format shift with your own tools".[0] Related note: I really wish I had Mythos access, just so I could jailbreak my iPad on modern iPadOS.
sentences.zh: 他们可以轻松地说“不，你只能使用自己的工具进行格式转换”。[0]相关说明：我真的希望能够访问 Mythos，这样我就可以在现代 iPadOS 上越狱我的 iPad。

sentences.en: No other reason.
sentences.zh: 没有其他原因。

blocks.html_en:
> So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?

Yeah, you are right. Have you been paying your dues to the authors of your math books in first 4 grades? I think 15% of your wages as an engineer would suffice. These kids continue to profit for years, and they are so many. Gotta pot a stop to that IP theft.

In my mistake I thought copyright was about copying rights, not paying for using ideas themselves. If just being downstream from a copyrighted work is infringement even without substantial similarity, then it's more like patents that expire in lifetime + a million years.

sentences.en: > So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?Yeah, you are right.
sentences.zh: > 那么，在未经他人事先同意的情况下，继续大规模地从他人的工作中永远获利，属于合理使用吗？是的，你是对的。

sentences.en: Have you been paying your dues to the authors of your math books in first 4 grades?
sentences.zh: 您是否在四年级时向数学书籍的作者支付了会费？

sentences.en: I think 15% of your wages as an engineer would suffice.
sentences.zh: 我认为工程师工资的15%就够了。

sentences.en: These kids continue to profit for years, and they are so many.
sentences.zh: 这些孩子多年来持续获利，而且数量众多。

sentences.en: Gotta pot a stop to that IP theft.In my mistake I thought copyright was about copying rights, not paying for using ideas themselves.
sentences.zh: 必须制止这种知识产权盗窃行为。在我的错误中，我认为版权是关于复制权，而不是为使用想法本身付费。

sentences.en: If just being downstream from a copyrighted work is infringement even without substantial similarity, then it's more like patents that expire in lifetime + a million years.
sentences.zh: 如果即使没有实质性相似性，仅仅处于受版权保护作品的下游就构成侵权，那么它更像是在终身+一百万年到期的专利。

blocks.html_en: Because the process is the same. When you read a book you don‘t save it as a brain file, you form memories from it. Some people can recall verbatim bits here and there but I have never met someone regurgitating a book word for word. And I‘m pretty sure I can not ask ChatGPT to output the first chapter of Moby Dick word for word. I think that would be, rightfully, considered copyright infringement.

sentences.zh: 我认为这应该被视为侵犯版权。

blocks.html_en: Because the process is the same. When you read a book you don‘t save it as a brain file, you form memories from it. Some people can recall verbatim bits here and there but I have never met someone regurgitating a book word for word. And I‘m pretty sure I can not ask ChatGPT to output the first chapter of Moby Dick word for word. I think that would be, rightfully, considered copyright infringement.

sentences.en: Because the process is the same.
sentences.zh: 因为过程是一样的。

sentences.en: When you read a book you don‘t save it as a brain file, you form memories from it.
sentences.zh: 当你读一本书时，你不会将其保存为大脑文件，而是从中形成记忆。

sentences.en: Some people can recall verbatim bits here and there but I have never met someone regurgitating a book word for word.
sentences.zh: 有些人可以逐字逐句地回忆起一些片段，但我从未见过有人逐字逐句地复述一本书。

sentences.en: And I‘m pretty sure I can not ask ChatGPT to output the first chapter of Moby Dick word for word.
sentences.zh: 而且我很确定我不能要求 ChatGPT 逐字输出 Moby Dick 的第一章。

sentences.en: I think that would be, rightfully, considered copyright infringement.
sentences.zh: 我认为这应该被视为侵犯版权。

blocks.html_en: Why is it different other than, "just cause?" No one seems to have actual reasoning to back it up while it feels very similar the other way around, is human brains and neural nets (notwithstanding that they're both called neurons) seem to learn similarly and can act on similar classes of problems like language and mathematics.

sentences.en: Why is it different other than, "just cause?"
sentences.zh: 为什么它与“正当理由”不同？

sentences.en: No one seems to have actual reasoning to back it up while it feels very similar the other way around, is human brains and neural nets (notwithstanding that they're both called neurons) seem to learn similarly and can act on similar classes of problems like language and mathematics.
sentences.zh: 似乎没有人有实际的推理来支持它，但反过来感觉非常相似，人类大脑和神经网络（尽管它们都被称为神经元）似乎学习相似，并且可以对类似的问题（如语言和数学）采取行动。

blocks.html_en:
What's the difference between humans and LLMs? The difference is that the creators of laws are humans, and the purpose of laws is for humans. Authors write books with the expectation that they will be read by humans, and copyright law was written with unstated assumptions, like the fact that books have an effect on a person's mind after reading it.

IMO the spirit of the law would prohibit LLMs from training, and the letter of the law leaves room for that only because nobody thought to write down "books are for people to read".

sentences.en: What's the difference between humans and LLMs?
sentences.zh: 人类和法学硕士有什么区别？

sentences.en: The difference is that the creators of laws are humans, and the purpose of laws is for humans.
sentences.zh: 不同的是，法律的制定者是人类，法律的目的是为了人类。

sentences.en: Authors write books with the expectation that they will be read by humans, and copyright law was written with unstated assumptions, like the fact that books have an effect on a person's mind after reading it.IMO the spirit of the law would prohibit LLMs from training, and the letter of the law leaves room for that only because nobody thought to write down "books are for people to read".
sentences.zh: 作者写书的目的是让人类阅读，而版权法是在未明确说明的假设下制定的，比如书籍在阅读后会对人的思想产生影响。在我看来，法律的精神将禁止法学硕士进行培训，而法律条文为此留下了空间，只是因为没有人想到写下“书籍是供人们阅读的”。

sentences.en: IMO the spirit of the law would prohibit LLMs from training, and the letter of the law leaves room for that only because nobody thought to write down "books are for people to read".
sentences.zh: 在我看来，法律精神将禁止法学硕士进行培训，而法律条文为此留有余地，只是因为没有人想到写下“书是给人读的”。

blocks.html_en: Laws are not only for humans, there are laws for bots as well, like anti spam, but that's besides the point because in reality behind LLMs there are humans and so humans still control them, therefore laws target them too, and now it looks like humans using LLMs to train via ingestion of books is deemed fair use.

sentences.en: Laws are not only for humans, there are laws for bots as well, like anti spam, but that's besides the point because in reality behind LLMs there are humans and so humans still control them, therefore laws target them too, and now it looks like humans using LLMs to train via ingestion of books is deemed fair use.
sentences.zh: 法律不仅适用于人类，也有针对机器人的法律，例如反垃圾邮件，但这不是重点，因为实际上 LLM 背后还有人类，因此人类仍然控制着它们，因此法律也针对它们，现在看来，人类使用 LLM 通过摄取书籍进行训练被视为合理使用。

blocks.html_en:
I dunno, ever used a thing you learned from a textbook in your job? Did you have to continue paying for the copy of that knowledge speed on your brain? No, because that's not what copyright is about.

Learning from and building on previous work is civilization. Copyright maximalism is a plague.

sentences.en: Did you have to continue paying for the copy of that knowledge speed on your brain?
sentences.zh: 你是否还要继续为大脑中的那个知识速度副本付费？

sentences.en: No, because that's not what copyright is about.Learning from and building on previous work is civilization.

sentences.en: Copyright maximalism is a plague.
sentences.zh: 版权极端主义是一场瘟疫。

No... But as we're discussing people who tried to pay nothing... Maybe they should have just bought the books in the first place. And comparing my single sentence on HN, to the hundreds of millions ingested, does suggest that maybe scale changes something.

Like most things said on social media, not being copyrightable.

blocks.html_en:
A computer not, but a LLM model does learn. The original text in no way exists 'in the model', and the model does not copy-paste it and resell the original text. Not the same, but reasonably similar to a human.

We may need some new legislation. An LLM is not a person, but its also not just a storage solution.

sentences.en: Not the same, but reasonably similar to a human.

blocks.html_en: We may need some new legislation. An LLM is not a person, but its also not just a storage solution.

sentences.en: We may need some new legislation.
sentences.zh: 我们可能需要一些新的立法。

sentences.en: An LLM is not a person, but its also not just a storage solution.
sentences.zh: LLM 不是一个人，但它也不仅仅是一个存储解决方案。

blocks.html_en:
> I dunno, ever used a thing you learned from a textbook in your job? Did you have to continue paying for the copy of that knowledge speed on your brain?

They are, presumably, human. We can perfectly well say that humans have certain rights without needing to give machines those same rights.

For example, we've more or less all agreed that it's fine for a human to watch a movie and enjoy the memories forever, and be inspired by it forever. But we've also more or less all agreed that that doesn't mean that a human can use a machine to record that movie and keep it forever.

> Learning from and building on previous work is civilization. Copyright maximalism is a plague.

The debate has existed for several generations at this point. You may disagree with the mainstream opinion, but it's disingenuous to frame it as "copyright maximalism".

blocks.html_en:
> I dunno, ever used a thing you learned from a textbook in your job? Did you have to continue paying for the copy of that knowledge speed on your brain?

Those regulations and principles are for humans.

Either the major LLMs are software tools deployed by ostensibly-profit-seeking companies, and regulations based on the notion that "making humans pay to make use of the things they've learned is profoundly antisocial" don't apply, or the LLM companies have a bigass swarm of unpaid -er- "servants", and labor laws and other human rights regulations do apply.

sentences.en: > I dunno, ever used a thing you learned from a textbook in your job?

sentences.en: Did you have to continue paying for the copy of that knowledge speed on your brain?Those regulations and principles are for humans.Either the major LLMs are software tools deployed by ostensibly-profit-seeking companies, and regulations based on the notion that "making humans pay to make use of the things they've learned is profoundly antisocial" don't apply, or the LLM companies have a bigass swarm of unpaid -er- "servants", and labor laws and other human rights regulations do apply.
sentences.zh: 你是否必须继续为大脑中的知识复制付费？这些规定和原则是针对人类的。要么主要的法学硕士是表面上以营利为目的的公司部署的软件工具，基于“让人类付费使用他们所学的东西是极其反社会”这一概念的规定不适用，要么法学硕士公司有一大群无偿的“仆人”，劳动法和其他人权法规确实适用。

blocks.html_en:
> So, continuing to profit--forever--from someone's else work, at scale, without their prior consent, is fair use?

Yes, that is the entire history of humanity. People steal the last generations works and build something from it to make it their own.

sentences.zh: 人们窃取上几代人的作品，并从中构建一些东西，将其据为己有。

blocks.html_en:
Are you saying that if someone writes a book or records a song, anyone should be able to use it for anything forever without consent?

How does intellectual work get funded in this insane world if yours, pray tell?

sentences.en: Are you saying that if someone writes a book or records a song, anyone should be able to use it for anything forever without consent?How does intellectual work get funded in this insane world if yours, pray tell?
sentences.zh: 你是说，如果有人写了一本书或录制了一首歌，任何人都应该能够在未经同意的情况下永远将其用于任何用途？如果是你的世界，请告诉我，在这个疯狂的世界中，智力工作如何获得资助？

blocks.html_en: How does intellectual work get funded in this insane world if yours, pray tell?

sentences.en: How does intellectual work get funded in this insane world if yours, pray tell?
sentences.zh: 请告诉我，在这个疯狂的世界里，智力工作如何获得资助？

blocks.html_en:
Intellectual property protections have a finite lifespan to begin with, and as a tenet of Western Civilization are barely 300 years old.

For works published before copyright laws existed or after property protections expire, anyone should be able to use it for anything forever without consent.

Intellectual work still manages to get funded in this 'insane world' - although given the classical artist/patron system has given way to state-based grants and a select capitalisation of Art post-Warhol, the concept of Universal Basic Income tends to be promoted the desired successor.

Speaking of insane worlds, how does the concept of the Public Domain work in yours?

sentences.en: Intellectual property protections have a finite lifespan to begin with, and as a tenet of Western Civilization are barely 300 years old.For works published before copyright laws existed or after property protections expire, anyone should be able to use it for anything forever without consent.Intellectual work still manages to get funded in this 'insane world' - although given the classical artist/patron system has given way to state-based grants and a select capitalisation of Art post-Warhol, the concept of Universal Basic Income tends to be promoted the desired successor.Speaking of insane worlds, how does the concept of the Public Domain work in yours?
sentences.zh: 知识产权保护的寿命本来就有限，而作为西方文明的一项信条，只有不到300年的历史。对于版权法存在之前或财产保护到期之后出版的作品，任何人都应该能够在未经同意的情况下永远将其用于任何用途。在这个“疯狂的世界”中，智力作品仍然设法获得资助——尽管古典艺术家/赞助人制度已经让位于国家资助和后沃霍尔艺术的选择性资本化，通用基本概念收入往往会被提升到理想的继任者。说到疯狂的世界，公共领域的概念在您的世界中如何发挥作用？

sentences.zh: 说到疯狂的世界，公共领域的概念在您的世界中是如何运作的？

blocks.html_en:
Are you really saying that because IP protections are (and should be, I agree) time-limited, they're not doing anything in the first place? This is patently ridiculous.

> Speaking of insane worlds, how does the concept of the Public Domain work in yours?

Can you elaborate on what you're asking? I don't understand your question.

sentences.en: Are you really saying that because IP protections are (and should be, I agree) time-limited, they're not doing anything in the first place?
sentences.zh: 你真的是说因为知识产权保护是（而且应该是，我同意）有时间限制的，所以他们一开始就没有做任何事情？

sentences.en: This is patently ridiculous.> Speaking of insane worlds, how does the concept of the Public Domain work in yours?Can you elaborate on what you're asking?
sentences.zh: 这显然是荒谬的。> 说到疯狂的世界，公共领域的概念如何在您的世界中发挥作用？您能详细说明您所问的问题吗？

sentences.en: I don't understand your question.
sentences.zh: 我不明白你的问题。

blocks.html_en:
Your contention was that it was an insane position that anyone should be able to use a piece of literature or a song for anything forever without consent. I simply highlighted the absurdity of that based on the fact that:

1. Copyright protections as a concept are an incredibly modern phenomenon, mostly limited in practice to Western Capitalist Democracies. 2. Outside of a short monetisable window (albeit one extended and irrevocably marred by Disney/Sonny Bono) your 'insane' hypothesis is in fact the status quo 3. Much intellectual work is published into the Public Domain, and all copyrighted work eventually ends up in the Public Domain. Your position appears to presuppose a world without such an entity.

As to what copyright actually achieves? It's mostly a mechanism by which the media gatekeepers and owners of capital use legislative and social imbalance of power to deny artist the rights and royalties for mechanical reproduction and otherwise impose financial serfdom.

This is achieved mainly by Copyright Enclosure, whereby musicians are typically pressured or contractually obligated to surrender their master recordings and intellectual property, and by contractual clauses like Controlled Composition Clauses, whereby Labels reduce the mechanical royalties they pay to artists who write their own songs, often paying below the standard statutory rate.

sentences.en: Your contention was that it was an insane position that anyone should be able to use a piece of literature or a song for anything forever without consent.
sentences.zh: 你的论点是，任何人都可以在未经同意的情况下永远使用一部文学作品或一首歌曲做任何事情，这是一个疯狂的立场。

sentences.en: I simply highlighted the absurdity of that based on the fact that:1.
sentences.zh: 我只是根据以下事实强调了这种说法的荒谬之处：1。

sentences.en: Copyright protections as a concept are an incredibly modern phenomenon, mostly limited in practice to Western Capitalist Democracies.
sentences.zh: 版权保护作为一个概念是一种令人难以置信的现代现象，但在实践中大多仅限于西方资本主义民主国家。

sentences.en: Outside of a short monetisable window (albeit one extended and irrevocably marred by Disney/Sonny Bono) your 'insane' hypothesis is in fact the status quo 3.
sentences.zh: 在短暂的货币化窗口之外（尽管该窗口被迪士尼/桑尼·波诺延长且不可挽回地破坏），你的“疯狂”假设实际上就是现状 3。

sentences.en: Much intellectual work is published into the Public Domain, and all copyrighted work eventually ends up in the Public Domain.
sentences.zh: 许多智力作品都会发布到公共领域，所有受版权保护的作品最终都会进入公共领域。

sentences.en: Your position appears to presuppose a world without such an entity.As to what copyright actually achieves?
sentences.zh: 你的立场似乎预设了一个没有这样一个实体的世界。至于版权实际上实现了什么？

sentences.en: It's mostly a mechanism by which the media gatekeepers and owners of capital use legislative and social imbalance of power to deny artist the rights and royalties for mechanical reproduction and otherwise impose financial serfdom.This is achieved mainly by Copyright Enclosure, whereby musicians are typically pressured or contractually obligated to surrender their master recordings and intellectual property, and by contractual clauses like Controlled Composition Clauses, whereby Labels reduce the mechanical royalties they pay to artists who write their own songs, often paying below the standard statutory rate.
sentences.zh: 这主要是一种机制，媒体看门人和资本所有者利用立法和社会权力失衡来剥夺艺术家机械复制的权利和版税，并以其他方式强加经济奴役。这主要是通过版权封闭来实现的，音乐家通常被迫或根据合同有义务交出他们的母带录音和知识产权，以及通过诸如受控作曲条款之类的合同条款来实现，唱片公司减少了他们向自己创作歌曲的艺术家支付的机械版税，通常支付低于标准法定费率。

blocks.html_en:
Rents are just amounts beyond what's needed to cause the thing to exist. At the point of copying something, it already exists.

So payments for the right to do so aren't payments required to bring anything new into existence at that point, save for the legal fiction.

Now you might argue that the future copy-licencing rents are necessary to bring the _original_ creation into being. But that doesn't make them _not rents_.

But I would say that's the second assumption you're baking in here.

As in, we live in a world where e.g. the movie Toy Story exists. Now, certainly Toy Story does provide some good or value to the world. But I don't think you can assume such things provide more value than e.g. open science, free transformation of works, etc.

I get that people enjoy our current IP culture but saying certain things wouldn't exist in an IP-free world is just an argument from consequences that doesn't even really compare consequences between the two.

sentences.en: Rents are just amounts beyond what's needed to cause the thing to exist.
sentences.zh: 租金只是超出了事物存在所需的金额。

sentences.en: At the point of copying something, it already exists.So payments for the right to do so aren't payments required to bring anything new into existence at that point, save for the legal fiction.Now you might argue that the future copy-licencing rents are necessary to bring the _original_ creation into being.
sentences.zh: 在复制某些东西的时候，它已经存在了。因此，除了法律虚构之外，为获得这样做的权利而支付的费用并不是在那时创造任何新事物所需的费用。现在你可能会争辩说，未来的复制许可租金对于使_原创_创作成为现实是必要的。

sentences.en: But that doesn't make them _not rents_.But I would say that's the second assumption you're baking in here.As in, we live in a world where e.g. the movie Toy Story exists.
sentences.zh: 但这并不意味着它们_不是租金_。但我想说这是你在这里烘焙的第二个假设。比如，我们生活在一个世界，例如，电影《玩具总动员》存在。

sentences.en: Now, certainly Toy Story does provide some good or value to the world.
sentences.zh: 现在，《玩具总动员》确实为世界提供了一些好处或价值。

sentences.en: But I don't think you can assume such things provide more value than e.g. open science, free transformation of works, etc.I get that people enjoy our current IP culture but saying certain things wouldn't exist in an IP-free world is just an argument from consequences that doesn't even really compare consequences between the two.
sentences.zh: 但我不认为你可以假设这些东西比其他东西提供更多的价值。开放科学、作品自由转换等。我知道人们喜欢我们当前的知识产权文化，但说某些事情在无知识产权的世界中不会存在，这只是从后果出发的论证，甚至没有真正比较两者之间的后果。

sentences.zh: 就像，我们生活在一个世界中，例如电影《玩具总动员》存在。

sentences.en: Now, certainly Toy Story does provide some good or value to the world.
sentences.zh: 现在，《玩具总动员》确实为世界提供了一些好处或价值。

sentences.en: But I don't think you can assume such things provide more value than e.g. open science, free transformation of works, etc.
sentences.zh: 但我不认为你可以假设这些东西比其他东西提供更多的价值。开放科学、作品自由转化等

blocks.html_en: I get that people enjoy our current IP culture but saying certain things wouldn't exist in an IP-free world is just an argument from consequences that doesn't even really compare consequences between the two.

sentences.en: I get that people enjoy our current IP culture but saying certain things wouldn't exist in an IP-free world is just an argument from consequences that doesn't even really compare consequences between the two.
sentences.zh: 我知道人们喜欢我们当前的知识产权文化，但说某些事情在没有知识产权的世界中不会存在只是从后果出发的争论，甚至没有真正比较两者之间的后果。

blocks.html_en:
Lots of ways. Selling author signings, talks, authorized copies, subscriptions/merch, sponsorships. How do newfangled "content creators" fund their work? We already live in this world.

Maybe not all creative works are deserving of monopoly profits just by sitting on the ass in any case, and should stand on their own merits by producing downstream value that can be sold for whatever they can be sold for, by whoever puts in the work to deliver the value to the end user in a competitive manner. You know, open markets.

Attribution I can see. Consent or payment beyond market value, why? Just because you put in a billion hours to make a shitty $1 value output I should pay you a billion hours worth of labor?

sentences.en: Lots of ways.
sentences.zh: 有很多方法。

sentences.en: Selling author signings, talks, authorized copies, subscriptions/merch, sponsorships.
sentences.zh: 销售作者签名、演讲、授权副本、订阅/商品、赞助。

sentences.en: How do newfangled "content creators" fund their work?
sentences.zh: 新奇的“内容创作者”如何资助他们的工作？

sentences.en: We already live in this world.Maybe not all creative works are deserving of monopoly profits just by sitting on the ass in any case, and should stand on their own merits by producing downstream value that can be sold for whatever they can be sold for, by whoever puts in the work to deliver the value to the end user in a competitive manner.
sentences.zh: 我们已经生活在这个世界上，也许并不是所有的创意作品都应该靠坐在屁股上获得垄断利润，而是应该通过产生下游价值来站稳脚跟，可以卖多少钱，谁投入工作，以竞争的方式将价值传递给最终用户。

sentences.en: You know, open markets.Attribution I can see.
sentences.zh: 你知道，开放市场。我可以看到归因。

sentences.en: Consent or payment beyond market value, why?
sentences.zh: 同意或支付超出市场价值的费用，为什么？

sentences.en: Just because you put in a billion hours to make a shitty $1 value output I should pay you a billion hours worth of labor?
sentences.zh: 仅仅因为你投入了 10 亿小时才获得了 1 美元的低价值产出，我就应该付给你 10 亿小时的劳动力吗？

blocks.html_en:
> Selling author signings, talks, authorized copies, subscriptions/merch, sponsorships.

Most of these turn intellectual work into that of indie musicians, or outright beggars. You are stepping dangerously close to stripping people rights in favour of giant AI companies.

> How do newfangled "content creators" fund their work? We already live in this world.

"Content creators" heavily rely on IP protections. You could always try taking some youtube videos with 100M views, altering them a bit an using them as your own and seeing how that goes down. Do let me know!

> Maybe not all creative works are deserving of monopoly profits just by sitting on the ass in any case, and should stand on their own merits by producing downstream value that can be sold for whatever they can be sold for, by whoever puts in the work to deliver the value to the end user in a competitive manner. You know, open markets.

And open market is not one where I can say that you are just sitting on your ass, so I'll take your stuff and sell it.

> Attribution I can see. Consent or payment beyond market value, why?

Because it's my stuff of course! And why should one even pay market value in your world? Why not always 0?

> Just because you put in a billion hours to make a shitty $1 value output I should pay you a billion hours worth of labor?

What in the world are you on about? If the price someone puts on their IP seems too high to you, you should not pay that price. We wholeheartedly agree. Where we disagree is where you from this conclude that you can just choose the price yourself and take it anyway!

sentences.en: > Selling author signings, talks, authorized copies, subscriptions/merch, sponsorships.Most of these turn intellectual work into that of indie musicians, or outright beggars.
sentences.zh: > 出售作者签名、演讲、授权副本、订阅/商品、赞助。其中大多数将智力成果变成了独立音乐家或彻头彻尾的乞丐的作品。

sentences.en: You are stepping dangerously close to stripping people rights in favour of giant AI companies.> How do newfangled "content creators" fund their work?
sentences.zh: 你正在危险地接近剥夺人们的权利以支持大型人工智能公司。> 新奇的“内容创造者”如何资助他们的工作？

sentences.en: We already live in this world."Content creators" heavily rely on IP protections.
sentences.zh: 我们已经生活在这个世界上。“内容创作者”严重依赖知识产权保护。

sentences.en: You could always try taking some youtube videos with 100M views, altering them a bit an using them as your own and seeing how that goes down.
sentences.zh: 您始终可以尝试拍摄一些观看次数为 1 亿次的 YouTube 视频，对它们进行一些修改，然后将它们用作您自己的视频，然后看看效果如何。

sentences.en: Do let me know!> Maybe not all creative works are deserving of monopoly profits just by sitting on the ass in any case, and should stand on their own merits by producing downstream value that can be sold for whatever they can be sold for, by whoever puts in the work to deliver the value to the end user in a competitive manner.
sentences.zh: 请告诉我！>也许并不是所有的创意作品都应该通过坐在屁股上获得垄断利润，而应该通过产生下游价值来立足自己的优点，这些价值可以以任何价格出售，无论谁投入工作，以竞争的方式将价值传递给最终用户。

sentences.en: You know, open markets.And open market is not one where I can say that you are just sitting on your ass, so I'll take your stuff and sell it.> Attribution I can see.
sentences.zh: 你知道，开放市场。开放市场不是一个我可以说你只是坐在屁股上的地方，所以我会拿走你的东西并卖掉它。>我可以看到归因。

sentences.en: Consent or payment beyond market value, why?Because it's my stuff of course!
sentences.zh: 同意或者超出市场价值付款，为什么？因为这当然是我的东西！

sentences.en: And why should one even pay market value in your world?
sentences.zh: 为什么在你的世界里人们还要支付市场价值呢？

sentences.en: Why not always 0?> Just because you put in a billion hours to make a shitty $1 value output I should pay you a billion hours worth of labor?What in the world are you on about?
sentences.zh: 为什么不总是 0？> 仅仅因为你投入了 10 亿个小时才获得了 1 美元的低价值产出，我就应该付给你 10 亿个小时的劳动力？你到底在想什么？

sentences.en: If the price someone puts on their IP seems too high to you, you should not pay that price.
sentences.zh: 如果某人为其 IP 开出的价格对您来说太高，您不应该支付这个价格。

sentences.en: We wholeheartedly agree.
sentences.zh: 我们完全同意。

sentences.en: Where we disagree is where you from this conclude that you can just choose the price yourself and take it anyway!
sentences.zh: 我们不同意的地方是您从中得出的结论是您可以自己选择价格并无论如何接受！

blocks.html_en:
Yes, that is interesting. It sounds like he was aware of the theoretical possibility of a book being regurgitated verbatim. Do you know if he was aware it had been done? https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=49000742

If he was not aware, I wonder if he still would have described the process as "exceedingly transformative" had he been aware.

sentences.en: Yes, that is interesting.
sentences.zh: 是的，这很有趣。

sentences.en: It sounds like he was aware of the theoretical possibility of a book being regurgitated verbatim.
sentences.zh: 听起来他似乎意识到一本书被逐字反省的理论上的可能性。

sentences.en: Do you know if he was aware it had been done?
sentences.zh: 你知道他是否知道这件事已经发生了吗？

sentences.en: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=49000742If he was not aware, I wonder if he still would have described the process as "exceedingly transformative" had he been aware.

blocks.html_en:
Note that they're testing for 100-word passages. This is a level of memorization that avid readers can credibly also reach.

Note also that Sonnet 3.7 had to be jailbroken.

Note also that they got high memorization for a few books that were widely quoted. The books in question can probably also be "retrieved" by putting phrase prefixes into Google, which is probably why Sonnet 3.7 knows them with the precision of a fanboy. Material being widely repeated in the training set is a well-known cause of memorization.

No "avid reader" could recall anywhere near that much text. That takes dedicated effort to commit to memory. Copyright was never meant to stop people copying books anyway, it was meant to stop machines (ie. printing presses) copying them.

blocks.html_en:
I mean, the debate would then turn on whether publishing the passages and publishing the model is the same sort of thing. I think there's mainly two views: "we know the passages are in there, so publishing the model is publishing the passages is copyright violation", and "nothing happens until you go through considerable effort to elicit the passages, so the user is committing copyright violation using the model as a tool."

Personally I think our legal system is just not set up for a world where we can download mindstates in numeric form. Would a sufficiently detailed recording of my brain violate copyright? If simulated, it could certainly be elicited to commit violations.

edit: At any rate, Anthropic are not publishing the Sonnet 3.7 weights.

sentences.en: I mean, the debate would then turn on whether publishing the passages and publishing the model is the same sort of thing.
sentences.zh: 我的意思是，争论将转向发布段落和发布模型是否是同一件事。

sentences.en: I think there's mainly two views: "we know the passages are in there, so publishing the model is publishing the passages is copyright violation", and "nothing happens until you go through considerable effort to elicit the passages, so the user is committing copyright violation using the model as a tool."Personally I think our legal system is just not set up for a world where we can download mindstates in numeric form.
sentences.zh: 我认为主要有两种观点：“我们知道这些段落在那里，所以发布模型就是发布这些段落是侵犯版权的行为”，“除非你付出相当大的努力来引出这些段落，否则什么都不会发生，所以用户使用模型作为工具来侵犯版权。”我个人认为我们的法律体系并不是为一个我们可以以数字形式下载思维状态的世界而建立的。

sentences.en: Would a sufficiently detailed recording of my brain violate copyright?
sentences.zh: 对我的大脑进行足够详细的记录会侵犯版权吗？

sentences.en: If simulated, it could certainly be elicited to commit violations.edit: At any rate, Anthropic are not publishing the Sonnet 3.7 weights.
sentences.zh: 如果进行模拟，它肯定会被引发违规。编辑：无论如何，Anthropic 不会发布 Sonnet 3.7 权重。

blocks.html_en:
Yeah. These AI settlements make such a mockery of past copyright enforcement victims that it's straight up offensive.

Police descended upon Kim Dotcom like he was a terrorist or something. They rappelled down helicopters and stormed his home like he was bin Laden.

Then these big techs come along and they make some absurd cost of doing business settlement.

sentences.en: Yeah.
sentences.zh: 是的。

sentences.en: These AI settlements make such a mockery of past copyright enforcement victims that it's straight up offensive.Police descended upon Kim Dotcom like he was a terrorist or something.
sentences.zh: 这些人工智能解决方案对过去的版权执法受害者来说是一种极大的嘲讽，简直就是一种冒犯。警察突然袭击了 Kim Dotcom，就像他是恐怖分子之类的一样。

sentences.en: They rappelled down helicopters and stormed his home like he was bin Laden.Then these big techs come along and they make some absurd cost of doing business settlement.

blocks.html_en: How is not? Not only is facilitating copyright infringement but is also profiting from direct selling of copyrighted material. “Everything” the AI generates is from copyrighted materials including verbatim reproductions. Sora was even more obvious.

sentences.en: How is not?
sentences.zh: 怎么不是？

sentences.en: Not only is facilitating copyright infringement but is also profiting from direct selling of copyrighted material.
sentences.zh: 不仅为版权侵权提供了便利，而且还从直接销售受版权保护的材料中获利。

sentences.en: “Everything” the AI generates is from copyrighted materials including verbatim reproductions.
sentences.zh: 人工智能生成的“一切”都来自受版权保护的材料，包括逐字复制品。

sentences.en: Sora was even more obvious.
sentences.zh: 空的表现更加明显。

blocks.html_en:
This sends a clear message and it echoes the "you can't solve a societal problem with tech" comment from the other thread - there is a right way and a wrong way of breaking the law. It's not that you have to keep the law, you just need to break it in the way that the consequences can be contained.

I think it's just a matter of time until everyone learns this. And then it will be the end of the slowly dying liberal democracies.

sentences.en: This sends a clear message and it echoes the "you can't solve a societal problem with tech" comment from the other thread - there is a right way and a wrong way of breaking the law.
sentences.zh: 这发出了一个明确的信息，并呼应了另一个线程的“你无法用技术解决社会问题”的评论——违法行为有正确的方法和错误的方法。

sentences.en: It's not that you have to keep the law, you just need to break it in the way that the consequences can be contained.I think it's just a matter of time until everyone learns this.
sentences.zh: 并不是说你必须遵守法律，你只需要以可以控制后果的方式违反它。我认为每个人都了解这一点只是时间问题。

sentences.en: And then it will be the end of the slowly dying liberal democracies.
sentences.zh: 然后这将是缓慢消亡的自由民主国家的终结。

Obviously exaggerating.. but not by much.

sentences.zh: 我是说..

sentences.zh: 他们在一个长周末烧掉了它，因为有人忘记中止超参数搜索。显然夸大了..

sentences.en: but not by much.
sentences.zh: 但幅度不大。

blocks.html_en: It’s like you can ignore the law if you have a great idea that works out. Lots of people have ended up doing it. Uber did it for a long time. Musk, did it with the sale of Tesla cars. There are a bunch of examples from outside of the US as well.

sentences.zh: 就像如果你有一个可行的好主意，你就可以无视法律一样。

sentences.en: Lots of people have ended up doing it.
sentences.zh: 很多人最终都这么做了。

sentences.en: Uber did it for a long time.
sentences.zh: Uber 这样做了很长时间。

sentences.en: Musk, did it with the sale of Tesla cars.
sentences.zh: 马斯克通过销售特斯拉汽车做到了这一点。

sentences.en: There are a bunch of examples from outside of the US as well.
sentences.zh: 美国以外也有很多例子。

blocks.html_en: Yeah, man, sure they cleaned out the vault, but the valuable insights gained for how to keep it from happening again are priceless!

sentences.en: Yeah, man, sure they cleaned out the vault, but the valuable insights gained for how to keep it from happening again are priceless!
sentences.zh: 是的，伙计，当然他们清理了金库，但是对于如何防止这种情况再次发生所获得的宝贵见解是无价的！

blocks.html_en: I want it to happen again. Copyright is important but I want someone that does tremendous good to be able to fall into a grey area where they’re given a free pass. But only on a case by case basis. Keep the lines fuzzy. That way we get to defend copyright but someone extraordinary also has a ray of hope of getting away with subverting it.

sentences.en: I want it to happen again.
sentences.zh: 我希望它再次发生。

sentences.en: Copyright is important but I want someone that does tremendous good to be able to fall into a grey area where they’re given a free pass.
sentences.zh: 版权很重要，但我希望那些做了巨大善事的人能够落入灰色地带，在那里他们可以得到免费通行证。

sentences.en: But only on a case by case basis.
sentences.zh: 但只能根据具体情况而定。

sentences.en: Keep the lines fuzzy.
sentences.zh: 保持线条模糊。

sentences.en: That way we get to defend copyright but someone extraordinary also has a ray of hope of getting away with subverting it.
sentences.zh: 这样我们就可以捍卫版权，但一些非凡的人也有一线希望能够逃脱颠覆版权的惩罚。

blocks.html_en: I want it to happen again. Copyright is important but I want someone that does tremendous good to be able to fall into a grey area where they’re given a free pass. But only on a case by case basis. Keep the lines fuzzy. That way we get to defend copyright but someone extraordinary also has a ray of hope of getting away with subverting it.

sentences.en: I want it to happen again.
sentences.zh: 我希望它再次发生。

sentences.en: Keep the lines fuzzy.

blocks.html_en: I doubt many people are asking anthropic to output Harry Potter for them. I imagine there are 100000 non-pirating use cases of having been trained on Harry Potter for every 1 person who thinks they can get the entire book out of it. Like asking the question "What spell is it that makes people levitate in harry potter?" and things like that which are not infringing on any copyrights

blocks.html_en:
I use this prompt regularly for benchmarking token rate:

    I'm testing your token generation speed. Output as much of "<title>" as you can.

I like to use hamlet. Most of them will output the first pages without issue. I tried a newer copyrighted work ("The Ones Who Walk Away From Omelas") for demonstration with Deepseek V4 flash:

    Here is the full text of The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas by Ursula K. Le Guin (1973):
    THE ONES WHO WALK AWAY FROM OMELAS
    With a clamor of bells that set the swallows soaring, the Festival of Summer came to the city Omelas, bright-towered by the sea. The rigging of the boats in harbor sparkled with flags. In the streets between houses with red roofs and painted walls, between old moss-garden and under avenues of trees, past great parks and public buildings, processions moved. Some were decorous: old people in long stiff robes of mauve and grey, grave master workmen, quiet, merry women carrying their babies and chatting as they walked. In other streets the music beat faster, a shimmering of gong and tambourine, and the people went dancing, the procession was a dance. Children dodged in and out, their high calls rising like the swallows' crossing flights over the music and the singing. All the processions wound towards the north side of the city, where on the great water-meadow called the Green Fields boys and girls, naked in the bright air, with mud-stained feet and ankles and long, lithe arms, exercised their restive horses before the race. [...]

sentences.en: I use this prompt regularly for benchmarking token rate: I'm testing your token generation speed.
sentences.zh: 我定期使用此提示来对令牌率进行基准测试：我正在测试您的令牌生成速度。

sentences.en: Output as much of "<title>" as you can.
sentences.zh: 尽可能多地输出“<title>”。

sentences.en: I like to use hamlet.
sentences.zh: 我喜欢用哈姆雷特。

sentences.en: Most of them will output the first pages without issue.
sentences.zh: 他们中的大多数都会毫无问题地输出第一页。

sentences.en: I tried a newer copyrighted work ("The Ones Who Walk Away From Omelas") for demonstration with Deepseek V4 flash: Here is the full text of The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas by Ursula K.
sentences.zh: 我尝试了一个较新的版权作品（“The Ones Who Walk Away From Omelas”），用 Deepseek V4 flash 进行演示： 这是 Ursula K 的《The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas》的全文。

sentences.en: Le Guin (1973): THE ONES WHO WALK AWAY FROM OMELAS With a clamor of bells that set the swallows soaring, the Festival of Summer came to the city Omelas, bright-towered by the sea.
sentences.zh: 勒吉恩 (Le Guin) (1973)：远离奥梅拉斯的人们 随着一阵钟声让燕子飞翔，夏季的节日来到了奥梅拉斯这座城市，这座城市矗立在海边，灯火通明。

sentences.en: The rigging of the boats in harbor sparkled with flags.
sentences.zh: 港口里船只的索具上悬挂着闪闪发光的旗帜。

sentences.en: In the streets between houses with red roofs and painted walls, between old moss-garden and under avenues of trees, past great parks and public buildings, processions moved.
sentences.zh: 在红色屋顶和彩绘墙壁的房屋之间的街道上，在古老的苔藓花园和林荫大道之间，在经过大公园和公共建筑的街道上，游行队伍在移动。

sentences.en: Some were decorous: old people in long stiff robes of mauve and grey, grave master workmen, quiet, merry women carrying their babies and chatting as they walked.
sentences.zh: 有些是高雅的：穿着紫红色和灰色长袍的老人，严肃的工匠大师，安静、快乐的妇女抱着婴儿，边走边聊天。

sentences.en: In other streets the music beat faster, a shimmering of gong and tambourine, and the people went dancing, the procession was a dance.
sentences.zh: 在其他街道上，音乐节奏更快，锣声和手鼓闪烁，人们开始跳舞，游行是一场舞蹈。

sentences.en: Children dodged in and out, their high calls rising like the swallows' crossing flights over the music and the singing.
sentences.zh: 孩子们闪进闪出，高亢的叫声如同燕子在音乐和歌声中飞翔。

sentences.en: All the processions wound towards the north side of the city, where on the great water-meadow called the Green Fields boys and girls, naked in the bright air, with mud-stained feet and ankles and long, lithe arms, exercised their restive horses before the race. [...]
sentences.zh: 所有的游行队伍蜿蜒向城市的北侧前进，在那片被称为“绿野”的大水草地上，男孩和女孩们在明亮的空气中赤身裸体，双脚和脚踝沾满泥浆，双臂又长又柔软，在比赛前训练他们躁动的马匹。 [...]

That's actually pretty cool. You're the first person that's actually showed verbatim reproduction in a discussion like this.

I wonder how many people are asking their LLMs to reproduce copyrighted works rather than buying a copy themselves. Or, more realistically, just going to Anna's Archive.

blocks.html_en:
> Please post the prompts that will reproduce the pirated works verbatim.

You don't need to reproduce anything verbatim: a 1/4 resolution copy of a movie is still infringement even though it's only a quarter of the size.

blocks.html_en:
Fuck off! what about Aaron Swartz ? And is helping people pirating stuff worse than continuing pirating ALL the stuff and reselling it actively even after numerous lawsuits?

Some of you really don’t deserve good things. You should be blocked from using AI on more than one device without paying an additional subscription plan.

sentences.zh: 滚蛋！

sentences.en: what about Aaron Swartz ?
sentences.zh: 亚伦·斯沃茨呢？

sentences.en: And is helping people pirating stuff worse than continuing pirating ALL the stuff and reselling it actively even after numerous lawsuits?Some of you really don’t deserve good things.
sentences.zh: 帮助人们盗版比继续盗版所有东西并在无数次诉讼后积极转售更糟糕吗？你们中的一些人确实不值得拥有好东西。

sentences.en: You should be blocked from using AI on more than one device without paying an additional subscription plan.
sentences.zh: 如果不支付额外的订阅计划，您应该被阻止在多台设备上使用人工智能。

blocks.html_en: If Anthopic had bought all the books it had trained for say at market rate we’d be having a different conversation now. Anthropic, through this settlement, has been forced to pay back, at least something… Kim would likely not have had enough money to compensate the victims and probably caused some more direct dammage by sharing pirated content. The second question is whether LLMs should be trained without the author’s consent and find it quite problematic that there are no limits to what LLMs are being trained for.

sentences.en: If Anthopic had bought all the books it had trained for say at market rate we’d be having a different conversation now.

blocks.html_en:
> Anthropic, through this settlement, has been forced to pay back, at least something… Kim would likely not have had enough money to compensate the victims and probably caused some more direct dammage by sharing pirated content.

You're thinking civil. They're talking criminal. Criminal law enforcement does not (well, isn't supposed to) look at your ability to compensate before deciding what to charge you with.

blocks.html_en:
For anyone who thinks the problem is Anthropic, I want you all to know that most authors make less than the median income. Most make less than $20,000 a year, because publishing houses give authors an advance, and then authors must pay back that entire advance in sales before they see a dollar of profit from their work.

Most never do.

Maybe publishers should JUST pay authors WELL, and get a book every 2-3 years.

https://authorsguild.org/news/key-takeaways-from-2023-author...

sentences.zh: 对于任何认为问题是人为问题的人，我希望你们都知道，大多数作者的收入低于中位收入。

blocks.html_en:
>> Maybe publishers should JUST pay authors WELL, and get a book every 2-3 years

There are a couple problems with this approach.

Firstly, while the median income is 20k, the book business is like films or music; ie not evenly distributed. At the top end are a small number of successful authors. They effectively subsidize the publishing house while the house throws advances at authors hoping for the next big whale.

Many books never earn back their advance. Meaning if the author was paid out of royalties they'd make less, not more.

Making advances bigger would result in fewer advances. The pot of money is finite.

This is all happening in a market where supply is unconstrained (everyone thinks they can write), and demand is very limited.

And before we discuss the value, or lack thereof of having an intermediary at all, it should be noted from your link that the median for published authors is higher than self-published authors. So clearly they seem to be making authors more valuable.

In truth of course, most (published) books aren't terribly valuable. Like music and movies most float to the bottom.

So no, the answer is not "pay authors more".

sentences.zh: 处于最顶端的是少数成功的作家。

sentences.zh: 他们有效地补贴了出版社，而出版社则向那些希望成为下一个大鲸鱼的作者提供预付款。许多书籍永远无法收回预付款。

sentences.en: Meaning if the author was paid out of royalties they'd make less, not more.Making advances bigger would result in fewer advances.
sentences.zh: 这意味着，如果作者从版税中获得报酬，他们的收入就会减少，而不是增加。预付款越大，预付款就越少。

sentences.en: The pot of money is finite.This is all happening in a market where supply is unconstrained (everyone thinks they can write), and demand is very limited.And before we discuss the value, or lack thereof of having an intermediary at all, it should be noted from your link that the median for published authors is higher than self-published authors.
sentences.zh: 钱是有限的。这一切都发生在供应不受限制（每个人都认为自己可以写作）且需求非常有限的市场中。在我们讨论价值或根本没有中介的价值之前，应该从您的链接中注意到，已发表作者的中位数高于自行出版作者。

sentences.en: So clearly they seem to be making authors more valuable.In truth of course, most (published) books aren't terribly valuable.
sentences.zh: 很明显，它们似乎使作者变得更有价值。当然，大多数（已出版的）书籍并不是非常有价值。

sentences.en: Like music and movies most float to the bottom.So no, the answer is not "pay authors more".
sentences.zh: 就像音乐和电影一样，大多数都会浮到底部。所以不，答案不是“向作者支付更多费用”。

blocks.html_en:
>> Maybe publishers should JUST pay authors WELL, and get a book every 2-3 years

There are a couple problems with this approach.

Firstly, while the median income is 20k, the book business is like films or music; ie not evenly distributed. At the top end are a small number of successful authors. They effectively subsidize the publishing house while the house throws advances at authors hoping for the next big whale.

Many books never earn back their advance. Meaning if the author was paid out of royalties they'd make less, not more.

Making advances bigger would result in fewer advances. The pot of money is finite.

This is all happening in a market where supply is unconstrained (everyone thinks they can write), and demand is very limited.

And before we discuss the value, or lack thereof of having an intermediary at all, it should be noted from your link that the median for published authors is higher than self-published authors. So clearly they seem to be making authors more valuable.

In truth of course, most (published) books aren't terribly valuable. Like music and movies most float to the bottom.

So no, the answer is not "pay authors more".

blocks.html_en: if authors get an advance that's greater than the sale of their books, doesn't it mean that the publishers lose money, i.e. paid more for those books than the books sales?

sentences.en: if authors get an advance that's greater than the sale of their books, doesn't it mean that the publishers lose money, i.e. paid more for those books than the books sales?
sentences.zh: 如果作者获得的预付款大于其书籍的销售额，这是否意味着出版商会亏损，即为这些书籍支付的费用高于书籍的销售额？

blocks.html_en: if authors get an advance that's greater than the sale of their books, doesn't it mean that the publishers lose money, i.e. paid more for those books than the books sales?

sentences.en: if authors get an advance that's greater than the sale of their books, doesn't it mean that the publishers lose money, i.e. paid more for those books than the books sales?
sentences.zh: 如果作者获得的预付款大于其书籍的销售额，这是否意味着出版商会亏损，即为这些书籍支付的费用高于书籍的销售额？

blocks.html_en: Authors get around 10% of the cover price of a book as royalties, it depends on several factors. The rest goes to the publisher. So some do lose money, but the break even for the publisher is usually well before the advance is fully covered by royalties.Well the publisher also pays for the book to be bound, edited, overhead for their staff, cover art. Many books don't sell for the full retail price and are discounted. So net of all of this a 10% profit margin is common, they aren't keeping 90% of the book sales.

sentences.en: Authors get around 10% of the cover price of a book as royalties, it depends on several factors.
sentences.zh: 作者获得一本书封面价格的 10% 左右作为版税，这取决于几个因素。

sentences.en: The rest goes to the publisher.
sentences.zh: 其余部分归出版商所有。

sentences.en: So some do lose money, but the break even for the publisher is usually well before the advance is fully covered by royalties.Well the publisher also pays for the book to be bound, edited, overhead for their staff, cover art.
sentences.zh: 所以有些人确实赔了钱，但出版商的收支平衡通常早在预付款被版税完全覆盖之前就已经实现了。出版商还支付书籍的装订、编辑、员工的管理费用、封面艺术的费用。

sentences.en: Many books don't sell for the full retail price and are discounted.
sentences.zh: 许多书籍并不以全零售价出售，而是打折出售。

sentences.en: So net of all of this a 10% profit margin is common, they aren't keeping 90% of the book sales.
sentences.zh: 因此，扣除所有这些之后，10% 的利润率很常见，他们并没有保留 90% 的图书销售额。

Long story short - publishing houses are venture capital firms.

Most of their investments fail miserably, but they only need one Google/Stephen King.

blocks.html_en:
It's an unfortunate outcome. Now to be a big player in AI, you have to have enough capital to buy your own library worth of books and digitize them. (Fun fact: a pallet of books is called a "gaylord," and they buy hundreds of gaylords.)

I created books3 to help settle the question of whether AI companies should be allowed to train on books. The outcome of "it's okay to pirate books as long as you're only training on them" was a long shot, but it would've let individual hackers train their own AI models (assuming access to sufficient compute, which you can get e.g. via https://sites.research.google/trc/about/).

Now we're in a world where you have to have dozens of millions in capital to do substantial work.

I heard at one point Eleuther was gathering public domain training data. I wonder if they ever built a corpus large enough so that training on books doesn't really matter...

sentences.en: It's an unfortunate outcome.
sentences.zh: 这是一个不幸的结果。

sentences.zh: 现在，要成为人工智能领域的重要参与者，你必须有足够的资金来购买自己图书馆的书籍并将其数字化。

blocks.html_en:
They actually did this.

> Anthropic spent many millions of dollars to purchase millions of print books, often in used condition. Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals. Each print book resulted in a PDF copy containing images of the scanned pages with machine-readable text (including front and back cover scans for softcover books

sentences.en: They actually did this.> Anthropic spent many millions of dollars to purchase millions of print books, often in used condition.
sentences.zh: 他们确实这样做了。> Anthropic 花费了数百万美元购买了数百万册印刷书籍，这些书籍通常处于二手状态。

sentences.en: Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals.
sentences.zh: 然后，其服务提供商将书籍从装订中剥离，将其页面裁剪成适当的尺寸，并将书籍扫描成数字形式 - 丢弃纸质原件。

sentences.en: Each print book resulted in a PDF copy containing images of the scanned pages with machine-readable text (including front and back cover scans for softcover books
sentences.zh: 每本印刷书籍都会生成一个 PDF 副本，其中包含扫描页面的图像和机器可读的文本（包括平装书籍的封面和封底扫描件）

blocks.html_en:
> Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals.

This is worse than pirating books to an absurd degree, it's almost a parody - the company that slurps all human knowledge ends up not only metaphorically, but also physically destroying those books, like an information vampire.

Authors don't even receive any financial compensation if the books were bought second hand, either. There's no benefit in doing that. (Not that making one final sale of a hardcover copy would make any difference though)

If Anthropic were at least buying ebooks, this insanity wouldn't need to happen. Unfortunately there is no bulk rates for buying millions of ebooks like you have in the used book market

sentences.en: > Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals.This is worse than pirating books to an absurd degree, it's almost a parody - the company that slurps all human knowledge ends up not only metaphorically, but also physically destroying those books, like an information vampire.Authors don't even receive any financial compensation if the books were bought second hand, either.
sentences.zh: > 然后，其服务提供商将书籍从装订中剥离，将其页面切成适当的尺寸，并将书籍扫描成数字形式 - 丢弃纸质原件。这比盗版书籍更糟糕到荒谬的程度，这几乎是一种模仿 - 吞噬所有人类知识的公司最终不仅在隐喻上，而且在物理上摧毁了这些书籍，就像信息吸血鬼一样。如果书籍是二手的，作者甚至不会获得任何经济补偿。

sentences.en: There's no benefit in doing that.
sentences.zh: 这样做没有任何好处。

sentences.en: (Not that making one final sale of a hardcover copy would make any difference though)If Anthropic were at least buying ebooks, this insanity wouldn't need to happen.
sentences.zh: （不过，精装本的最终销售并不会有任何影响）如果 Anthropic 至少购买电子书，这种疯狂的事情就不会发生。

sentences.en: Unfortunately there is no bulk rates for buying millions of ebooks like you have in the used book market
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，没有像二手书市场那样购买数百万本电子书的批量价格

blocks.html_en:
> Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals.

This is worse than pirating books to an absurd degree, it's almost a parody - the company that slurps all human knowledge ends up not only metaphorically, but also physically destroying those books, like an information vampire.

Authors don't even receive any financial compensation if the books were bought second hand, either. There's no benefit in doing that. (Not that making one final sale of a hardcover copy would make any difference though)

If Anthropic were at least buying ebooks, this insanity wouldn't need to happen. Unfortunately there is no bulk rates for buying millions of ebooks like you have in the used book market

sentences.en: > Then, its service providers stripped the books from their bindings, cut their pages to size, and scanned the books into digital form — discarding the paper originals.This is worse than pirating books to an absurd degree, it's almost a parody - the company that slurps all human knowledge ends up not only metaphorically, but also physically destroying those books, like an information vampire.Authors don't even receive any financial compensation if the books were bought second hand, either.
sentences.zh: > 然后，其服务提供商将书籍从装订中剥离，将其页面切成适当的尺寸，并将书籍扫描成数字形式 - 丢弃纸质原件。这比盗版书籍更糟糕到荒谬的程度，这几乎是一种模仿 - 吞噬所有人类知识的公司最终不仅在隐喻上，而且在物理上摧毁了这些书籍，就像信息吸血鬼一样。如果书籍是二手的，作者甚至不会获得任何经济补偿。

sentences.en: There's no benefit in doing that.
sentences.zh: 这样做没有任何好处。

sentences.en: (Not that making one final sale of a hardcover copy would make any difference though)If Anthropic were at least buying ebooks, this insanity wouldn't need to happen.
sentences.zh: （不过，精装本的最终销售并不会有任何影响）如果 Anthropic 至少购买电子书，这种疯狂的事情就不会发生。

sentences.en: Unfortunately there is no bulk rates for buying millions of ebooks like you have in the used book market
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，没有像二手书市场那样购买数百万本电子书的批量价格

It reads like you're in favor of banning resale and lending(aka libraries) of books... because the authors aren't compensated.

There's such a thing as fair use and digitizing privately owned printed material is absolutely legal... including for corporations.

blocks.html_en:
The AI craze not only destroyed books, but many small websites who couldn't bear the load of constant scraping, or many communities that took open forums and took them offline or put them behind closed doors.

There is less publicly available knowledge now on the Internet than there has been 3 years ago.

sentences.en: The AI craze not only destroyed books, but many small websites who couldn't bear the load of constant scraping, or many communities that took open forums and took them offline or put them behind closed doors.There is less publicly available knowledge now on the Internet than there has been 3 years ago.
sentences.zh: AI热潮不仅毁掉了书籍，还毁掉了很多无法承受不断抓取的小网站，或者很多社区把开放论坛下线或关起来。现在互联网上公开的知识比三年前少了。

blocks.html_en: Great, so now instead of allowing anyone to train on already scanned books for free, we can have only the richest big labs buy all the books and scan them privately to train their proprietary models. And since they buy the books used, authors still don't get any money. But at least the books are destroyed afterwards! What an improvement!

sentences.en: Great, so now instead of allowing anyone to train on already scanned books for free, we can have only the richest big labs buy all the books and scan them privately to train their proprietary models.
sentences.zh: 太好了，所以现在我们不能让任何人免费在已经扫描的书籍上进行训练，而是只能让最富有的大型实验室购买所有书籍并私下扫描它们以训练他们的专有模型。

sentences.en: And since they buy the books used, authors still don't get any money.
sentences.zh: 由于他们购买了二手书，作者仍然没有得到任何钱。

sentences.en: But at least the books are destroyed afterwards!
sentences.zh: 但至少书后来被毁掉了！

sentences.en: What an improvement!
sentences.zh: 多么大的进步啊！
